[3] - Zhao YY., "Traditional uses, phytochemistry, pharmacology, pharmacokinetics and quality control of Polyporus umbellatus.", J Ethnopharmacol, 2013, PMID: 23811047
[4] - Li H., "Renoprotective effect and mechanism of polysaccharide from Polyporus umbellatus sclerotia.", Carbohydr Polym, 2019, PMID: 30832835
[5] - Liu T., "Polyporus umbellatus polysaccharide iron-based nanocomposite for synergistic M1 polarization and tumor cell death.", Int J Biol Macromol, 2023, PMID: 37586629

narrative_summary.historical_use: Widely consumed as food in East Asia for centuries. Known in Japan as 'Enokitake', in China as 'Jin Zhen Gu' (golden needle mushroom). Used in TCM for liver disease, gastric disorders, and as a tonic. The wild form (brown, thick-stemmed) differs significantly in bioactive content from the commercially cultivated white, slender variety.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Polysaccharides from F. velutipes demonstrate immunomodulatory, antioxidant, and anti-tumour activities with diverse structural and functional properties [1][5]. Renoprotective effects have been documented in chronic kidney disease models [2]. Protective activity against cisplatin-induced acute kidney injury has been observed in preclinical settings [3]. Flammutoxin, a cardiotoxic protein present in the raw mushroom, is inactivated by cooking — an important safety consideration [4]. Ergothioneine content is among the highest of edible mushrooms, contributing to its antioxidant profile [1].
narrative_summary.side_effects: Raw mushroom consumption can cause GI irritation and potential cardiotoxicity (flammutoxin) — cooking is required to inactivate flammutoxin. Cooked or dried preparations are well tolerated based on long-standing culinary use; rare hypersensitivity reactions have been reported.
narrative_summary.contraindications: Raw consumption contraindicated. Rare mushroom allergy. No other well-established contraindications at dietary levels.

clinical_data.used_part: Fruiting body (cooked or extracted)
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: FVE activates macrophages and NK cells; stimulates Th1 cytokines (IFN-γ, IL-2). Flammulin inhibits protein synthesis in cancer cells via ribosome inactivating protein (RIP)-like activity. Lectins act as TLR-4 ligands, activating innate immunity. Proflamin targets CD4+ T-cells as an immunomodulatory lectin.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: Absorption is compound-dependent. L-ergothioneine, which is present in Flammulina velutipes, is avidly absorbed after oral administration in healthy humans, with rapid and significant elevations in plasma and whole blood concentrations confirmed in a controlled clinical trial [1]. No human absorption data exist for FVE (immunomodulatory protein), flammulin, or the beta-glucan fractions — No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Ergothioneine absorbed via organic cation transporter OCTN1; accumulates selectively in tissues under oxidative stress. FVE and flammulin are proteins largely degraded during digestion; immunological activity may be mediated at mucosal level.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: L-ergothioneine distributes extensively into whole blood and erythrocytes after oral dosing in humans, accumulating via the OCTN1 transporter [1]. Distribution of FVE protein and polysaccharide fractions has not been characterised in humans — No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — FVE (Flammulina velutipes lectin) and high-MW polysaccharides primarily act at mucosal surfaces and GALT. Flammulin distributes preferentially to actively dividing cells. Ergothioneine accumulates in erythrocytes via the OCTN1 transporter. No formal human PK distribution data.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: L-ergothioneine undergoes partial in vivo biotransformation in humans; whole blood concentrations of hercynine and S-methyl-ergothioneine correlate with ergothioneine levels following oral dosing [1]. No human metabolic data exist for other Flammulina velutipes constituents — No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Ergothioneine: not significantly metabolised; retained in erythrocytes and mitochondria-rich tissues. Protein bioactives: digested to peptides/amino acids.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: L-ergothioneine is largely retained by the body after oral dosing in humans; urinary excretion accounts for less than 4% of the administered dose [1]. Renal and biliary excretion of other constituents has not been characterised in humans — No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Ergothioneine: renal excretion of intact molecule and metabolites.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Lack of clinical studies prevents interaction grading. Unknown safety profile with concomitant drug use.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: Flammutoxin is heat-labile and destroyed at cooking temperatures (>70°C); cooked preparations are non-toxic. LD50 of whole cooked extract >5 g/kg in rodents. No organ toxicity documented with cooked/extracted forms.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Dietary consumption of cooked mushroom considered safe. Raw consumption must be avoided. Concentrated extracts — insufficient data.
special_precautions.lactation: Dietary cooked consumption likely safe. No data on concentrated extract transfer to breast milk.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: No hepatotoxicity documented with cooked preparations. Standard monitoring with concentrated extracts.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: No specific risk identified. Ergothioneine is renally excreted; accumulation possible in severe renal failure — monitor.

cited_references: [1] - Ye S., "Research progress and future development potential of Flammulina velutipes polysaccharides.", Int J Biol Macromol, 2024, PMID: 38599436
[2] - Lee MM., "Renoprotective Effects of Brown-Strain Flammulina velutipes Singer in Chronic Kidney Disease.", Int J Mol Sci, 2024, PMID: 39596166
[3] - Tu Y., "Flammulina Velutipes polysaccharides ameliorate cisplatin-induced acute kidney injury in mice.", Int J Biol Macromol, 2025, PMID: 39706410
[4] - Mustonen AM., "Myo- and cardiotoxic effects of the wild winter mushroom (Flammulina velutipes) on mice.", Exp Biol Med (Maywood), 2018, PMID: 29495884
[5] - Łysakowska P., "Medicinal Mushrooms: Their Bioactive Components, Nutritional Value and Application in Functional Food Production-A Review.", Molecules, 2023, PMID: 37513265

narrative_summary.historical_use: Consumed as food across Europe and Asia for centuries. Less prominent in traditional medicine than Asian medicinal fungi, but used in Eastern European folk medicine for general health. One of the most widely cultivated mushrooms globally, appreciated for culinary and nutritional value. References to medicinal use in Slavic folk medicine for liver and cardiovascular conditions.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Among edible fungi, oyster mushrooms have the most clinical evidence for lipid-lowering effects; a review of clinical and preclinical studies confirms cardiometabolic benefits [1][2]. Consumption of P. ostreatus extract has been associated with improvements in blood parameters and cytokine profiles in clinical studies [3]. Lovastatin (mevinolin) content — a genuine statin naturally occurring in the fruiting body — is the likely cholesterol-lowering mechanism [2]. Immunomodulatory properties and anti-cancer activity are documented in preclinical and review literature [4][5]. Pleuran (beta-1,3/1,6-glucan) from P. ostreatus is commercially used as an immunomodulatory supplement [4].
narrative_summary.side_effects: Generally very well tolerated. Rare contact dermatitis (occupational). Rare asthma with spore inhalation. Possible GI intolerance with very large consumption.
narrative_summary.contraindications: Statins (atorvastatin, simvastatin) — additive lipid-lowering effect and potential for myopathy due to lovastatin content. Caution in patients with myopathy. Immunosuppressants — pharmacodynamic antagonism (pleuran).

clinical_data.used_part: Fruiting body
primary_active_compounds: Lovastatin (mevinolin) — 2.8% dry weight in some varieties
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Lovastatin competitively inhibits HMG-CoA reductase, reducing hepatic cholesterol synthesis and upregulating LDL receptor expression. Beta-glucans activate Dectin-1, promoting Th1 immune response. ACE-inhibitory peptides reduce angiotensin II formation, producing antihypertensive effects. Ergothioneine acts as antioxidant via Nrf2/HO-1 upregulation.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: Absorption is compound-dependent. Lovastatin, present at up to 2.8% dry weight in some Pleurotus ostreatus varieties, has very low oral bioavailability in humans (~5%) due to extensive first-pass metabolism at both intestinal and hepatic levels; this is well-characterised in human clinical pharmacokinetic studies [1]. Ergothioneine, also present in oyster mushroom, is avidly absorbed after oral administration in healthy humans with significant plasma and whole blood elevations [2]. No human bioavailability data exist for pleuran (beta-glucan) or other polysaccharide fractions — No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Lovastatin absorbed in prodrug (lactone) form; hydrolysed to active hydroxy acid in gut/liver; bioavailability ~5% due to first-pass effect. Pleuran: limited systemic absorption; active at mucosal/GALT level.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: Lovastatin is extensively distributed to hepatic tissue (target organ) following absorption; high plasma protein binding (>95%) limits free drug concentrations [1]. Ergothioneine distributes into whole blood and erythrocytes via the OCTN1 transporter after oral dosing in humans [2]. Distribution of pleuran and other polysaccharides has not been characterised in humans — No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Lovastatin undergoes extensive hepatic first-pass metabolism; the active β-hydroxy acid metabolite distributes systemically with high hepatic uptake (target organ) and ~95% plasma protein binding. Ergothioneine concentrates in erythrocytes via OCTN1 transporter. Polysaccharide fractions confined to GI/GALT. Volume of distribution of lovastatin ~0.5 L/kg.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: Lovastatin is a prodrug (inactive lactone) hydrolysed to the active hydroxy acid form; it undergoes extensive CYP3A4-mediated first-pass metabolism at intestinal and hepatic levels, accounting for its low oral bioavailability [1]. Ergothioneine is partially biotransformed in vivo to hercynine and S-methyl-ergothioneine, as measured in human whole blood after oral dosing [2]. No human metabolic data exist for pleuran or other polysaccharide fractions — No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Lovastatin extensively metabolised by CYP3A4 to active and inactive metabolites. Pleuran fermented by gut microbiota.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: Lovastatin and its metabolites are excreted primarily via bile into faeces (~83%); renal excretion accounts for approximately 10% [1]. Ergothioneine is largely retained in the body; urinary excretion is less than 4% of the administered oral dose in humans [2]. Renal or biliary excretion of pleuran has not been characterised in humans — No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Lovastatin: primarily biliary/fecal (83%); minor renal (10%). Pleuran: fecal.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Lack of clinical studies prevents interaction grading. Unknown safety profile with concomitant drug use.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: Whole mushroom: LD50 not established; dietary consumption is safe. Concentrated lovastatin-rich extracts carry statin-class adverse effects (myopathy, elevated liver enzymes) at pharmacological doses.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Dietary consumption of culinary amounts carries no documented risk based on long traditional food use; no teratogenicity has been demonstrated at food-level doses. However, Lovastatin (a secondary metabolite present at low levels in the fruiting body) is contraindicated during pregnancy (Category X statin classification — teratogenic in animal models). High-dose concentrated extracts should be avoided due to insufficient clinical safety data during pregnancy.
special_precautions.lactation: Lovastatin excreted in breast milk. Concentrated lovastatin-containing preparations should be avoided during breastfeeding.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: Lovastatin is contraindicated in active liver disease. Monitoring of liver enzymes with regular high-dose consumption is prudent.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: Lovastatin primarily excreted biliary; mild renal impairment generally does not require dose adjustment. Severe renal impairment — use with caution.

cited_references: [1] - Dicks L., "Effect of the Intake of Oyster Mushrooms (Pleurotus ostreatus) on Cardiometabolic Parameters-A Systematic Review.", Nutrients, 2020, PMID: 32316680
[2] - Araújo PL., "Pleurotus Mushrooms in Nutrition and Health: Clinical and Preclinical Insights for Nutraceutical Applications.", Compr Rev Food Sci Food Saf, 2025, PMID: 40899490
[3] - Dündar A., "Effect of Pleurotus ostreatus Water Extract Consumption on Blood Parameters and Cytokine Values in Healthy Volunteers.", J Am Nutr Assoc, 2024, PMID: 38935369
[4] - Motta F., "Mushrooms and immunity.", J Autoimmun, 2021, PMID: 33276307
[5] - Gariboldi MB., "Anti-Cancer Potential of Edible/Medicinal Mushrooms in Breast Cancer.", Int J Mol Sci, 2023, PMID: 37373268

narrative_summary.historical_use: Consumed as food in parts of Asia, Africa, and Latin America. Used in traditional medicine in Mexico and Southeast Asia for fever, headache, and infections. One of the most widespread mushroom species globally (found on every continent except Antarctica). Not traditionally prominent in Chinese or Japanese medicinal systems, but significant in modern Japanese oncology research.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Schizophyllan (SPG / Sonifilan / sizofiran) — a beta-1,3-glucan — is approved in Japan as an adjunct to radiation therapy in cervical cancer, with clinical trials demonstrating improved survival [1]. Efficacy as an immunotherapy adjunct has also been studied in advanced gastric cancer [2]. Immunomodulatory mechanisms include enhancement of NK cell and macrophage activity [3]. Importantly, S. commune is also an opportunistic pathogen capable of causing allergic bronchopulmonary mycosis and sino-pulmonary infections in immunocompromised hosts [4][5]. Antifungal properties are noted in vitro.
narrative_summary.side_effects: IV/intratumoral administration: local injection reactions, mild fever. Oral use: generally well tolerated. Note: can cause opportunistic infections (schizophyllosis) in severely immunocompromised patients.
narrative_summary.contraindications: Severe immunocompromise (risk of opportunistic infection). Immunosuppressants — complex pharmacodynamic interaction.

clinical_data.used_part: Fruiting body; SPG (schizophyllan) derived from fermentation of mycelium
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Schizophyllan activates macrophages and NK cells via Dectin-1 receptor; upregulates IL-2, IFN-γ, TNF-α. Augments T-lymphocyte and dendritic cell function. Anti-tumour mechanism involves host-mediated immunostimulation rather than direct cytotoxicity. TLR-2/Dectin-1 co-activation amplifies innate immune signalling cascades.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Schizophyllan (SPG/sizofiran) is approved for clinical use in Japan as an IV immunomodulator in cervical cancer; oral bioavailability data for polysaccharide fractions remain unavailable. In vivo biodegradation and metabolism of beta-glucans reviewed in Chen X et al. (2022), Front Vet Sci [1]. Oral bioavailability of high-MW schizophyllan is very limited; clinical use is parenteral (intramuscular or intratumoral). Oral formulations active at mucosal immune level.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Schizophyllan (SPG) is a high-molecular-weight β-glucan; following clinical (IV/IM) administration in Japan, it distributes to spleen, lymph nodes, and tumour-associated lymphatics. Oral bioavailability is poor; clinically relevant distribution data applies to parenteral routes only. No meaningful systemic oral distribution.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Partially hydrolysed by gut glucanases; absorbed fractions undergo hepatic metabolism.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Primarily fecal for oral forms; renal for parenteral metabolites.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Lack of clinical studies prevents interaction grading. Unknown safety profile with concomitant drug use.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: SPG parenteral: well-tolerated in clinical trials; injection site reactions. Risk of opportunistic infection (Schizophyllum commune sino-pulmonary infection) in immunocompromised individuals — this is a documented pathogen, not just a theoretical risk.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Insufficient human clinical data. Avoid during pregnancy.
special_precautions.lactation: No data available. Avoid during breastfeeding.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: No hepatotoxicity documented. Standard monitoring recommended.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: No specific risk identified. No dose adjustment data.

cited_references: [1] - Okamura K., "Clinical evaluation of schizophyllan combined with irradiation in patients with cervical cancer.", Cancer, 1986, PMID: 2941141
[2] - Fujimoto S., "Clinical efficacies of schizophyllan (SPG) on advanced gastric cancer.", Nihon Geka Gakkai Zasshi, 1989, PMID: 2531270
[3] - Borchers AT., "Mushrooms, tumors, and immunity.", Proc Soc Exp Biol Med, 1999, PMID: 10460691

[5] - Hoenigl M., "Global guideline for the diagnosis and management of rare mould infections: an initiative of the European Confederation of Medical Mycology.", Lancet Infect Dis, 2021, PMID: 33606997

narrative_summary.historical_use: Used in Traditional Chinese Medicine for over 1,000 years, known as 'Mu Er' (wood ear) or 'Hei Mu Er' (black wood ear). Traditionally used for improving blood circulation, treating throat conditions, and as a yin tonic for the lungs and stomach. A common food ingredient in Asian cuisine (soups, stir-fries). Reference to its use for blood disorders found in Ben Cao Gang Mu (Li Shizhen, 1596).
narrative_summary.modern_application: Clinically relevant for its significant anticoagulant and antiplatelet effects — in vitro screening has confirmed potent platelet inhibition by Auricularia extracts [1]. Antidiabetic effects via alpha-glucosidase inhibition and anti-proliferative activity against cancer cell lines are documented in preclinical research [2][4]. Polysaccharide-rich preparations have been evaluated in polyherbal combination formulas for immune support [3]. Immunomodulatory polysaccharides (AAP) exhibit multiple bioactivities in preclinical models [5]. Caution is warranted when consumed in large quantities alongside anticoagulant medication due to additive platelet inhibition [1].
narrative_summary.side_effects: Significant anticoagulant/antiplatelet effect — risk of bleeding, especially with concurrent anticoagulant use. GI upset with large quantities. Rare hypersensitivity.
narrative_summary.contraindications: Concurrent use with anticoagulants or antiplatelets (warfarin, aspirin, clopidogrel, heparin) — clinically significant bleeding risk. Pre-surgical period — discontinue at least 2 weeks before surgery. Haemophilia or bleeding disorders.

clinical_data.used_part: Fruiting body
primary_active_compounds: Auricularia auricula polysaccharides (AAP — beta-glucans, acidic heteropolysaccharides)
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Adenosine and AAP inhibit platelet aggregation via adenosine A2A receptor activation, increasing platelet cAMP and reducing thromboxane A2. Anticoagulant polysaccharides inhibit thrombin and factor Xa (heparin-like mechanism). Hypoglycaemic activity via α-glucosidase inhibition and GLUT4 upregulation in skeletal muscle. Immunomodulation via TLR-4/Dectin-1.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Oral absorption of ME-2, a homogeneous polysaccharide from Auricularia auricula-judae, was systematically investigated using a dual-labeling pharmacokinetic approach; the study quantified absorption characteristics and intestinal uptake kinetics [1]. Adenosine rapidly absorbed and metabolised. Polysaccharide fractions limited systemic absorption; active at mucosal level. Human PK data for whole extract not available.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Tissue distribution of the Auricularia polysaccharide ME-2 was characterised following oral administration using the same dual-labeling PK study [1], revealing organ-specific accumulation patterns. Adenosine distributes rapidly to red blood cells and vascular endothelium via active transport; plasma half-life ~10 seconds. High-MW anticoagulant polysaccharides distribute to vascular endothelium. Ergosterol distributes to lipid-rich membranes. No formal human PK studies for the fungal extract as a whole.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Adenosine rapidly deaminated by adenosine deaminase to inosine. Polysaccharides fermented by gut microbiota.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Renal excretion of adenosine metabolites; fecal for polysaccharides.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Lack of clinical studies prevents interaction grading. Unknown safety profile with concomitant drug use.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: Well tolerated at dietary amounts based on extensive culinary use. At pharmacological concentrations, the antiplatelet effect is the primary clinical concern; caution is warranted with concurrent anticoagulant therapy. No significant organ toxicity has been identified in animal studies.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Anticoagulant properties raise concern for increased bleeding risk during pregnancy, particularly peripartum. Avoid concentrated preparations; dietary quantities with medical supervision.
special_precautions.lactation: No data on breast milk excretion. Dietary use likely safe; concentrated preparations — insufficient data.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: No hepatotoxicity documented. If hepatic coagulopathy is present, additive bleeding risk from antiplatelet effect — use with caution.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: No specific risk identified. Adenosine metabolism may be affected in severe renal impairment; monitor.

cited_references: [1] - Poniedziałek B., "The Effect of Mushroom Extracts on Human Platelet and Blood Coagulation: In vitro Screening of Eight Edible Species.", Nutrients, 2019, PMID: 31842490

[2] - Wong JH., "Mushroom extracts and compounds with suppressive action on breast cancer: evidence from studies using cultured cancer cells, tumor-bearing animals, and clinical trials.", Appl Microbiol Biotechnol, 2020, PMID: 32274562

[3] - Nakyam T., "The Polyherbal Functional Ingredient Containing Ginger, Chinese Date, and Wood Ear Mushroom (Auricularia auricula-judae) Promotes Immune System Activity.", Biomed Res Int, 2023, PMID: 37564141
[4] - Panthong S., "Antioxidant activity, anti-proliferative activity, and amino acid profiles of ethanolic extracts of edible mushrooms.", Genet Mol Res, 2016, PMID: 27813595
[5] - Łysakowska P., "Medicinal Mushrooms: Their Bioactive Components, Nutritional Value and Application in Functional Food Production-A Review.", Molecules, 2023, PMID: 37513265

narrative_summary.historical_use: Eaten as a culinary mushroom in Europe and North America, known as the shaggy ink cap. Not historically prominent in TCM but used in Chinese folk medicine under the name 'Ji Tui Mo'. Recognised for its edibility (must be consumed promptly after harvest as it auto-digests rapidly). Traditional use in Chinese folk medicine for improved digestion and energy.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Studied primarily for antidiabetic properties; ethanolic extracts demonstrate significant hypoglycaemic and antioxidant activity in animal models [1][3]. Mycelium polysaccharides have shown anti-diabetic nephropathic activity in preclinical studies [4]. A comprehensive review documents both the functional properties and potential safety concerns — including a disulfiram-like reaction with alcohol mediated by coprine [2]. Anti-inflammatory and antiarthritic properties are under early investigation via nanogel formulations [5].
narrative_summary.side_effects: Well tolerated when consumed cooked, with rare GI symptoms reported. Coprine (found in related Coprinus species — notably C. atramentarius, NOT C. comatus) causes a disulfiram-like reaction with alcohol; this compound is absent in C. comatus. Species identification is critical — do not confuse with related species.
narrative_summary.contraindications: Species confusion risk (C. atramentarius contains coprine — disulfiram-like reaction with alcohol). C. comatus itself does not contain coprine. Antidiabetic drugs — potential additive hypoglycaemia. No other established contraindications.

clinical_data.used_part: Fruiting body
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Polysaccharides stimulate insulin secretion from pancreatic beta-cells and improve insulin sensitivity via PPAR-γ activation; inhibit α-glucosidase and aldose reductase. Lectins act as TLR-4 agonists, promoting macrophage activation. Ink-cap polysaccharides upregulate Nrf2/HO-1, providing antioxidant protection in hyperglycaemic conditions. Coprinol exhibits antimicrobial activity against MRSA.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: Absorption is compound-dependent. L-ergothioneine, found in Coprinus comatus, is avidly absorbed after oral administration in healthy humans, producing significant and sustained elevations in plasma and whole blood concentrations [1]. No human oral absorption data exist for vanadium, coprinin, or the beta-glucan fractions — No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Vanadium absorption: 0.5–2% of oral dose; accumulates in bone, liver, kidney. Polysaccharide fractions: limited systemic absorption, active at mucosal level. Human PK data for whole extract unavailable.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: L-ergothioneine distributes extensively to whole blood and erythrocytes in humans via the OCTN1 transporter [1]. Vanadium pharmacokinetics in humans were studied following intravenous exposure; the metal distributes rapidly to erythrocytes and bone, with a multi-compartment disposition profile [2]. Oral vanadium distribution data are not available. No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Polysaccharide fractions concentrate at GALT, pancreatic tissue, and spleen, consistent with antidiabetic and immunomodulatory activity. Vanadium-containing compounds (if present) distribute to bone and kidney. No formal human pharmacokinetic distribution data available for this species.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: L-ergothioneine is partially biotransformed in humans to hercynine and S-methyl-ergothioneine, as confirmed by whole blood measurements after oral dosing [1]. No human metabolic data exist for vanadium (oral) or other Coprinus constituents — No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Vanadium: reduction from V(V) to V(IV) in gut; hepatic metabolism. Polysaccharides: gut microbial fermentation.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: L-ergothioneine is strongly retained by the body; urinary excretion is less than 4% of the administered oral dose in healthy humans [1]. Vanadium is excreted renally following IV exposure in humans, with a terminal half-life of several days due to bone sequestration [2]. No oral excretion data for vanadium in a dietary context are available — No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Vanadium: primarily renal; polysaccharides: fecal.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Lack of clinical studies prevents interaction grading. Unknown safety profile with concomitant drug use.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: LD50 of whole extract >5 g/kg (oral, rodents). Vanadium toxicity: high doses (not achievable from dietary mushroom) cause GI irritation, renal accumulation; irrelevant at culinary quantities. No clinically significant organ toxicity documented.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Insufficient data on concentrated extracts. Dietary consumption likely safe. Vanadium at pharmacological doses is not recommended in pregnancy.
special_precautions.lactation: No data on breast milk excretion. Dietary consumption likely safe; concentrated extracts — insufficient data.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: No hepatotoxicity documented. Standard monitoring recommended.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: Vanadium excreted renally; accumulation possible in renal impairment. Avoid high-dose concentrated preparations in renal failure.

cited_references: [1] - Ratnaningtyas NI., "Ethanol extract of the mushroom Coprinus comatus exhibits antidiabetic and antioxidant activities in streptozotocin-induced diabetic rats.", Pharm Biol, 2022, PMID: 35675226
[2] - Nowakowski P., "The two faces of Coprinus comatus-Functional properties and potential hazards.", Phytother Res, 2020, PMID: 32462723
[3] - Husen F., "Antidiabetic Effects and Antioxidant Properties of the Saggy Ink Cap Medicinal Mushroom, Coprinus comatus.", Int J Med Mushrooms, 2021, PMID: 34595888
[4] - Gao Z., "Characterization and anti-diabetic nephropathic ability of mycelium polysaccharides from Coprinus comatus.", Carbohydr Polym, 2021, PMID: 33142624
[5] - Ratnaningtyas NI., "Therapeutic potential of Coprinus comatus nanogels: Antiarthritic and anti-inflammatory effects in Freund's adjuvant-induced arthritis.", Vet World, 2025, PMID: 40342737

narrative_summary.historical_use: Endemic to Taiwan, where it grows exclusively on the inner cavity of Cinnamomum kanehirai (Taiwanese sassafras). Known locally as 'Zhang Zhi' or 'Niu Zhang Zhi'. Used in Taiwanese indigenous medicine and folk medicine for centuries as a treatment for liver disease, abdominal pain, diarrhoea, and as a hangover remedy. Historically one of the most expensive medicinal fungi in Taiwan.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Hepatoprotective and anti-tumour properties are the primary research focus. Clinical evidence supports hepatoprotective activity in patients with non-alcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH) [1] and in sub-health adults [2]. Polysaccharide intervention modulates clinical symptoms likely via gut microbiome regulation [3]. Anti-proliferative activity against prostate cancer via immune modulation has been documented [4]. Hepatoprotective mechanisms are further supported by preclinical data showing reduction of hepatotoxicity in rodent models [5]. Antroquinonol, a unique ubiquinone derivative, has entered Phase II clinical trials in Taiwan.
narrative_summary.side_effects: Generally well tolerated. Mild GI symptoms. Rare hypersensitivity. Potential QT interval prolongation with high doses (preclinical).
narrative_summary.contraindications: Hepatotoxic drugs — monitor liver function. Anticoagulants — theoretical additive effect. Drugs affecting QT interval — use with caution (preclinical signal).

clinical_data.used_part: Fruiting body and mycelium
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Antroquinonol inhibits Ras/Akt/mTOR signalling, inducing tumour cell autophagy and apoptosis; also inhibits HIF-1α, reducing hypoxia-driven VEGF expression (anti-angiogenic). Triterpenoids inhibit NF-κB pathway, reducing IL-6, TNF-α, and COX-2. Hepatoprotective via Nrf2/HO-1 upregulation and suppression of CYP2E1-mediated oxidative stress.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Intestinal absorption of 14 ergostane and lanostane triterpenoids from Antrodia cinnamomea was characterised using a bidirectional Caco-2 cell monolayer model; most triterpenoids showed low-to-moderate intestinal permeability, consistent with their high lipophilicity [1]. No human oral bioavailability data are available. Antroquinonol bioavailability ~20% in rodents. Triterpenoid absorption limited; improved by lipid-based formulation. Human PK data emerging from Phase I/II trials.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Antroquinonol and ubiquinone derivatives are highly lipophilic; distribute to tumour vasculature, liver, and adipose tissue. Significant hepatic first-pass metabolism expected. Half-life of antroquinonol estimated at ~4–6 hours in preclinical models. No formal human PK distribution data available.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Hepatic CYP3A4-mediated metabolism of triterpenoids. Antroquinonol undergoes phase II conjugation.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Biliary/fecal for triterpenoids; renal for water-soluble metabolites.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Lack of clinical studies prevents interaction grading. Unknown safety profile with concomitant drug use.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: LD50 >5 g/kg for whole extract (rodents). Antroquinonol demonstrated an acceptable tolerability profile in Phase I clinical evaluation. QT prolongation was observed at suprapharmacological doses in preclinical studies — clinical significance at therapeutic doses is unclear; long-term safety data are limited to early-phase clinical experience.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Insufficient clinical data. Potent bioactivity warrants avoidance during pregnancy.
special_precautions.lactation: No data on breast milk excretion. Avoid during breastfeeding.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: Paradox: hepatoprotective in documented trials, but CYP metabolism may be impaired; monitor carefully and start at low doses.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: No specific data. Standard monitoring recommended.

narrative_summary.historical_use: One of the oldest known medicinal fungi, used by humans for at least 5,300 years — a piece was found in the toolkit of Ötzi the Iceman (Alpine mummy, c. 3,300 BCE), likely used as a fire-starting material and possibly for medicinal purposes. Used in European folk medicine for wound healing, as a haemostatic agent, and to treat digestive disorders. The amadou (processed inner tissue) was used historically as tinder, clothing, and wound dressings.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Contemporary research has confirmed antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and cytotoxic properties. Insoluble extracted fibres from the fruiting body demonstrate immunological activity relevant to nutraceutical applications [1]. Melanin-glucan complex shows anti-infective properties against bacterial and viral pathogens [2]. Ethanol extracts inhibit cell growth and induce apoptosis in cancer cell lines in vitro [3]. Antimicrobial activity against a range of pathogens has been confirmed in vitro [4]. Fermented extracellular polysaccharides exhibit antioxidant properties [5]. Human clinical data remain absent; biomaterial and wound-care applications are an emerging research focus.
narrative_summary.side_effects: Insufficient human clinical data to characterise adverse effects fully. No significant toxicity reported in preclinical studies. Theoretical bleeding risk with concentrated extracts.
narrative_summary.contraindications: No well-established clinical contraindications have been identified, primarily due to the absence of human clinical data. Anticoagulants — theoretical additive anticoagulant activity warrants avoidance of concurrent use. Use during pregnancy and lactation is not recommended due to insufficient clinical safety data.

clinical_data.used_part: Fruiting body (conk), particularly the inner trama tissue (amadou)
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Hispidin and fomentariol act as potent antioxidants (radical scavenging via phenolic hydroxyl groups). Fomentariol inhibits topoisomerase II, inducing cancer cell cycle arrest (G2/M phase). Polysaccharide fractions activate Dectin-1/TLR-4, promoting IL-12 and TNF-α secretion. Anticoagulant activity through inhibition of thrombin and platelet aggregation.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: Absorption is compound-dependent. Protocatechuic acid (PCA), a phenolic acid present in Fomes fomentarius alongside caffeic acid, is absorbed in healthy humans after oral dosing; plasma concentrations were quantified following single and multiple oral doses in 12 healthy subjects [1]. No human data exist for fomentariol, hispidin, or the fomentosan polysaccharide — No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — No human PK data available. Phenolic compounds expected to have moderate oral absorption; triterpenoids limited due to poor water solubility.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Fomentariol and hispidin are phenolic compounds with moderate lipophilicity; distribute systemically after intestinal absorption. Polysaccharide fractions concentrate in GALT and spleen. No formal human pharmacokinetic distribution data; all available data from in vitro and rodent studies.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Assumed hepatic phase I/II metabolism of phenolic and triterpenoid compounds based on analogous structures.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: Urinary excretion of protocatechuic acid was measured in healthy human subjects, enabling characterisation of renal clearance as part of a full single- and multiple-dose PK study [1]. No human excretion data exist for other Fomes fomentarius constituents — No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Assumed primarily biliary/fecal for triterpenoids; renal for phenolic metabolites.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Lack of clinical studies prevents interaction grading. Unknown safety profile with concomitant drug use.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: LD50 not established in humans. No significant organ toxicity in preclinical studies at normal doses. Amadou (physical material) used as wound dressing is non-toxic.

special_precautions.pregnancy: No human clinical safety data are available. Use during pregnancy is not recommended due to the absence of established safety information.
special_precautions.lactation: No human clinical safety data are available. Excretion of constituents into breast milk has not been studied. Use during breastfeeding is not recommended due to insufficient safety information.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: No hepatotoxicity documented. No data in hepatic impairment.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: No data available. Standard caution applies.

cited_references: [1] - Kalitukha L., "Medicinal Potential of the Insoluble Extracted Fibers Isolated from the Fomes fomentarius Fruiting Body.", Int J Med Mushrooms, 2023, PMID: 37017659
[2] - Seniuk OF., "Anti-infective properties of the melanin-glucan complex obtained from medicinal tinder bracket mushroom, Fomes fomentarius.", Int J Med Mushrooms, 2011, PMID: 22135899
[3] - Lee SO., "Fomes fomentarius Ethanol Extract Exerts Inhibition of Cell Growth and Motility Induction through the Suppression of Focal Adhesion Kinase.", Int J Mol Sci, 2019, PMID: 30845749
[4] - Doğan HH., "Fomes fomentarius and Tricholoma anatolicum (Agaricomycetes) Extracts Exhibit Significant Antimicrobial Activity.", Int J Med Mushrooms, 2020, PMID: 32478999
[5] - Zhang Q., "Optimization of fermentation of Fomes fomentarius extracellular polysaccharide and antioxidant activity.", Cell Mol Biol (Noisy-le-grand), 2020, PMID: 33287923

narrative_summary.historical_use: The taxonomically correct name for the wild-harvested Tibetan caterpillar fungus, previously classified as Cordyceps sinensis. Formed from parasitism of Hepialus moth larvae at altitudes >3,500 m on the Tibetan Plateau. Documented in Tibetan medical texts since the 15th century as a potent tonic for fatigue, sexual vitality, respiratory capacity, and kidney function. Among the most expensive natural substances per gram globally.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Clinically validated in Chinese RCTs for chronic kidney disease — combined use with ACE inhibitors or ARBs has demonstrated reductions in proteinuria and creatinine stabilisation in diabetic nephropathy [1][2] and general CKD [3]. Systematic review data support inclusion in integrative oncology discussions, though evidence quality varies [4]. Because wild harvesting is unsustainable and prohibitively expensive, nearly all modern research uses cultured Cs-4 mycelium (often still labelled 'Cordyceps sinensis') [5]. Anti-fatigue and VO2-max improvement in elderly subjects have been documented with cultured preparations [5].
narrative_summary.side_effects: Mild GI upset. Rare hypersensitivity. Potential hypoglycaemia with antidiabetic co-administration. Bleeding risk with anticoagulants.
narrative_summary.contraindications: Anticoagulants. Immunosuppressants (cyclosporine). Autoimmune conditions — caution. Pre-surgery.

clinical_data.used_part: Whole fruiting body-larvae complex (wild); Cs-4 mycelium (cultured, commercially used)
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Cordycepin inhibits mRNA polyadenylation, blocking cancer cell proliferation. Adenosine receptor modulation regulates cardiac output and vascular tone. HIF-1α stabilisation improves tissue oxygenation under hypoxic conditions. Cordycepin activates AMPK, enhancing glucose uptake in muscle. Th1/Th2 balance regulation via IL-12/IL-10 axis modulation.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — D-Mannitol, a naturally occurring sugar alcohol enriched in Ophiocordyceps sinensis, is absorbed after oral ingestion in humans; plasma PK was characterised in a multicentre clinical study using doses of 50–150 g (pharmacological laxative doses, substantially higher than dietary amounts from mushroom sources) [1]. No human oral absorption data exist for cordycepin, adenosine, or polysaccharide fractions at nutritional doses. Same profile as cultured Cordyceps — cordycepin rapidly deaminated. Unique wild-type peptides: oral bioavailability likely very low due to GI degradation.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Cordycepin distributes rapidly to lung, kidney, liver, and immune tissues. CSE-PS (polysaccharide) concentrates in spleen and thymus. Adenosine has a very short plasma half-life (~10 seconds) due to active cellular uptake. Plasma protein binding of nucleoside compounds is generally low (<30%). Pharmacokinetics closely parallel those of Cordyceps sinensis (Cs-4).
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Metabolic pathway data for cordycepin (primary bioactive compound) available in Lee JB et al. (2019), Sci Rep [2]: cordycepin is rapidly deaminated to 3'-deoxyinosine by adenosine deaminase; a novel nucleoside rescue pathway enables oral bioactivity despite this rapid first-pass degradation. Hepatic metabolism; adenosine deaminase-mediated for cordycepin.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Renal excretion of purine metabolites.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Cyclosporine (the immunosuppressant) — potential pharmacodynamic interaction as the fungus contains cyclosporin analogues. Anticoagulants, antidiabetics — same as Cordyceps sinensis.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: Wild form: same general profile as Cs-4. Heavy metal contamination has been documented in some wild specimens from polluted regions — an important quality concern. Cs-4 cultured mycelium has a characterised tolerability profile from clinical studies; the wild form has limited controlled safety data and additional quality concerns regarding contamination.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Insufficient data. Avoid during pregnancy.
special_precautions.lactation: Insufficient data. Avoid during breastfeeding.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: No hepatotoxicity documented. Standard monitoring recommended.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: Studied as renoprotective agent in CKD. Monitor renal function during use. Caution with severe impairment.

cited_references: [1] - Yan G., "The effects of Ophiocordyceps sinensis combined with ACEI/ARB on diabetic kidney disease: A systematic review and meta-analysis.", Phytomedicine, 2023, PMID: 36375237
[2] - Xue X., "Ophiocordyceps sinensis preparations combined with the renin-angiotensin system inhibitor in treating diabetic nephropathy.", Front Pharmacol, 2024, PMID: 38716236

[3] - Luo Y., "Use of Ophiocordyceps sinensis (syn. Cordyceps sinensis) combined with angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor therapy for treatment of IgA nephropathy.", Ren Fail, 2015, PMID: 25682973
[4] - Narayanan S., "Medicinal Mushroom Supplements in Cancer: A Systematic Review of Clinical Studies.", Curr Oncol Rep, 2023, PMID: 36995535
[5] - Łysakowska P., "Medicinal Mushrooms: Their Bioactive Components, Nutritional Value and Application in Functional Food Production-A Review.", Molecules, 2023, PMID: 37513265

narrative_summary.historical_use: Known in Traditional Chinese Medicine as 'Wu Ling Shen' (the fungus found inside the chambers of ancient termite mounds). Used historically for insomnia, anxiety, and to calm the mind. Referenced in TCM pharmacopeias as a sedative tonic. Grows inside the nests of Odontotermes formosanus (Formosan subterranean termite) in a unique symbiotic relationship.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Studied primarily for sedative, anxiolytic, and neuroprotective properties; metabolites, biological activities, and clinical applications have been comprehensively reviewed [1]. A multicenter randomised controlled trial documented significant antidepressant effects in epilepsy patients receiving X. nigripes extract alongside standard therapy [2]. Anti-inflammatory polysaccharide fractions exhibit synergistic activity in vitro [3]. Novel resorcinol derivatives [4] and sesquiterpenoids [5] with neuroprotective activities have been isolated from the fruiting body. Commercial preparations are marketed in Taiwan and China for insomnia and anxiety, though broader clinical data remain limited [1].
narrative_summary.side_effects: Insufficient human clinical data. Potential sedation and CNS depression. No significant toxicity reported in preclinical studies.
narrative_summary.contraindications: CNS depressants (benzodiazepines, alcohol, barbiturates, opioids) — potential additive sedation. Caution when operating machinery. Insufficient data for comprehensive contraindication profile.

clinical_data.used_part: Sclerotium found inside termite mounds
primary_active_compounds: N/A — specific active compounds not yet fully characterised
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Positive allosteric modulation of GABA-A receptor (benzodiazepine-like effect), prolonging inhibitory chloride channel opening. 5-HT reuptake inhibition proposed in animal models. Anti-inflammatory effects via NF-κB inhibition and reduction of IL-6, TNF-α. Adenosine receptor modulation may contribute to anxiolytic and sleep-promoting effects.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — No human PK data available. Bioavailability of active constituents not characterised.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Active alkaloids and sesquiterpenes are lipophilic; cross the blood-brain barrier in animal studies and distribute broadly to CNS, with preference for limbic structures (amygdala, hippocampus). No formal human PK distribution data; all data from rodent sleep and anxiety models.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Not established.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Not established.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: CNS depressants — additive sedative effect (theoretical, based on GABA-A mechanism). Alcohol — potential potentiation of CNS depression. No drug interaction studies in humans.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: LD50 not established for human use. Preclinical animal studies show no significant organ toxicity at doses up to 5 g/kg. Human safety data absent.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Insufficient data. Avoid during pregnancy.
special_precautions.lactation: Insufficient data. Avoid during breastfeeding.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: No clinical data are available in hepatic impairment. No hepatotoxicity has been identified in preclinical studies. The GABA-A modulating activity of nigrigenin may theoretically be potentiated in hepatic encephalopathy due to altered GABAergic tone; use with caution and standard monitoring.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: No clinical data are available in renal impairment. No specific nephrotoxicity has been identified in preclinical studies. Standard monitoring is recommended; renal excretion pathway for nigrigenin metabolites has not been characterised.

cited_references: [1] - Liang AL., "A comprehensive review of the metabolites, biological activities, and clinical application of Xylaria nigripes.", J Ethnopharmacol, 2025, PMID: 40436127
[2] - Peng WF., "The anti-depression effect of Xylaria nigripes in patients with epilepsy: A multicenter randomized controlled study.", Seizure, 2015, PMID: 26076841
[3] - Jen CI., "Synergistic anti-inflammatory effects of different polysaccharide components from Xylaria nigripes.", J Food Biochem, 2021, PMID: 33687093
[4] - Li LQ., "Four new resorcinol derivatives with neuroprotective activities from Xylaria nigripes.", Nat Prod Res, 2022, PMID: 33715538
[5] - Long HP., "Five brasilane-type sesquiterpenoids with neuroprotective activities from Xylaria nigripes.", Phytochemistry, 2025, PMID: 39662694

narrative_summary.historical_use: A fungal pathogen of maize (Zea mays), consumed as a food delicacy in Mesoamerica for thousands of years, known as 'huitlacoche' or 'cuitlacoche' in Mexico. Considered a luxury food by the Aztecs and still used in Mexican haute cuisine. Medicinal use documented in indigenous Mexican and Central American traditions for digestive complaints and as a tonic. The infected corn galls are consumed as a vegetable.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Research focus is on its exceptional nutritional profile — high in lysine, unsaturated fatty acids, and fibre — as well as bioactive compounds including ustilagic acid, which shows anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties in vitro [1][2]. Study as an edible and functional mushroom alongside more established medicinal species is ongoing [2]. Cellular stress response mechanisms, including responses to pharmacological agents, have been characterised at the molecular level [3]. Basidiocarp development and the molecular basis of the edible form have been investigated [4]. Indole compounds including indole-3-acetic acid are under investigation for potential anti-tumour activity [5]. Human clinical data for medicinal applications are absent.
narrative_summary.side_effects: Ustilago maydis has a long culinary history in Mesoamerican cuisines without documented serious adverse effects; this historical food use does not constitute clinical evidence of tolerability at pharmacological doses. Allergic reactions are possible in persons with corn or mould allergies. No significant toxicity has been documented in available literature.
narrative_summary.contraindications: Corn or mould allergy. No established pharmacological contraindications.

clinical_data.used_part: Infected maize galls (fruiting/smut tissue)
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Ustilagic acid disrupts bacterial and fungal cell membranes (detergent-like mechanism) and inhibits NF-κB-mediated pro-inflammatory signalling. Ergothioneine accumulates via OCTN1 transporter; protects mitochondria and DNA from oxidative damage. Polysaccharides activate Dectin-1/TLR-4, promoting macrophage differentiation. Rich in melanin precursors with UV-protective properties.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: Absorption is compound-dependent. Protocatechuic acid (PCA) and gallic acid, both phenolic acids present in Ustilago maydis, are absorbed in healthy humans after oral dosing; plasma concentrations were quantified in 12 healthy subjects (6 women, 6 men) following single and multiple oral doses [1]. No human oral absorption data exist for ustilagic acid, indole-3-acetic acid, or other major Ustilago constituents — No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — No human PK data for isolated bioactives. Food matrix absorption of phenolics is moderate. Indole compounds absorbed via passive diffusion.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Ustilagic acid and lipid compounds are highly lipophilic; distribute to lipid-rich membranes and adipose tissue. Ergothioneine distributes preferentially to erythrocytes, kidney, liver, and eye via the OCTN1 transporter. Polysaccharides confined to GI/GALT. No formal PK distribution data.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Hepatic conjugation of phenolic compounds; indole metabolism via CYP1A2.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: Urinary excretion of protocatechuic acid was measured in the same 12-subject clinical PK study [1], enabling renal clearance characterisation. No human excretion data exist for other Ustilago maydis constituents — No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Renal excretion of conjugated phenolics.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: No documented drug interactions. CYP1A2 substrates — theoretical interaction via indole metabolism.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: Consumed as food for millennia at culinary quantities without documented serious adverse effects; this historical food use does not constitute formal clinical evidence of tolerability at pharmacological doses. No LD50 data exist for concentrated extracts. No organ toxicity has been identified in available studies.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Dietary consumption as a traditional food has not been associated with documented reproductive harm; no teratogenicity data exist for culinary amounts. Concentrated medicinal preparations lack clinical safety data and are not recommended during pregnancy.
special_precautions.lactation: Dietary consumption has a long culinary tradition in Mesoamerican cuisines without documented adverse effects during lactation; however, no controlled clinical safety data during breastfeeding exist. Concentrated preparations are not recommended due to insufficient data.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: No hepatotoxicity documented. Standard precautions for concentrated preparations.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: No specific risk identified.

cited_references: [1] - Wu HC., "Chemical Constituents and Bioactive Principles from the Mexican Truffle and Fermented Products of Ustilago maydis.", J Agric Food Chem, 2023, PMID: 36597352

[2] - González-Ibáñez L., "Edible and medicinal mushrooms (Pleurotus ostreatus, Ustilago maydis, Ganoderma lucidum) reduce oxidative stress and modify their antioxidant mechanisms.", Food Funct, 2023, PMID: 37161495
[3] - Soberanes-Gutiérrez CV., "Cell death in Ustilago maydis: comparison with other fungi and the effect of metformin and rapamycin.", FEMS Yeast Res, 2020, PMID: 32945857
[4] - León-Ramírez CG., "Transcriptomic analysis of basidiocarp development in Ustilago maydis (DC) Cda.", Fungal Genet Biol, 2017, PMID: 28285895
[5] - Cuamatzi-Flores J., "Enhanced oxidative stress resistance in Ustilago maydis and its implications on the virulence and pathogenesis.", Int Microbiol, 2024, PMID: 38401003

narrative_summary.historical_use: Used in Mesoamerican indigenous ceremonial and religious contexts for thousands of years, particularly by the Mazatec people of Oaxaca, Mexico (known as 'teonanácatl' — 'flesh of the gods'). Psilocybin was first isolated and synthesised by Albert Hofmann at Sandoz in 1958. Studied in the 1960s before being classified as Schedule I in the US in 1970, halting research for decades.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Psilocybin (the prodrug) and psilocin (the active metabolite) are the subject of renewed rigorous clinical research. A landmark N Engl J Med RCT demonstrated psilocybin to be non-inferior to escitalopram for depression [1]. A JAMA Psychiatry RCT demonstrated significant, durable antidepressant effects with two-dose psilocybin-assisted therapy for major depressive disorder [2]. A JAMA RCT confirmed single-dose efficacy for major depressive disorder [3]. A randomised controlled trial demonstrated significant response in treatment-resistant depression [4]. A meta-analysis confirmed consistent antidepressant effects across multiple RCTs [5]. FDA Breakthrough Therapy Designation has been granted for treatment-resistant depression and MDD.
narrative_summary.side_effects: Acute (dose-dependent): perceptual distortions, synesthesia, ego dissolution, anxiety, confusion, tachycardia, hypertension, nausea. Challenging psychological experiences ('bad trips') — risk mitigated by psychological support/set-and-setting. Headache post-session. Hallucinogen persisting perception disorder (HPPD) — rare. No physical withdrawal syndrome. Non-addictive.
narrative_summary.contraindications: Personal or family history of schizophrenia, bipolar I disorder, or other psychotic disorders — absolute contraindication (risk of precipitating psychosis). Concurrent lithium — risk of seizures and hyperthermia (documented case reports). MAOIs — risk of serotonin syndrome. Uncontrolled cardiovascular disease — relative contraindication (transient hypertension/tachycardia).

clinical_data.used_part: Fruiting body (as pharmacological source); isolated psilocybin used in clinical trials
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Psilocybin is rapidly dephosphorylated to psilocin by alkaline phosphatase. Psilocin is a full agonist at serotonin 5-HT2A receptor in cortical pyramidal neurons, disrupting default mode network (DMN) activity. Also agonist at 5-HT2C and 5-HT1A. Promotes neuroplasticity via BDNF upregulation and synaptogenesis. AMPA/NMDA receptor modulation contributes to sustained antidepressant effect.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: Well-characterised in humans across multiple clinical studies. Psilocybin is a prodrug; it is rapidly dephosphorylated to the active metabolite psilocin by alkaline phosphatase in the intestinal mucosa, liver, and plasma within minutes of ingestion. Psilocin reaches peak plasma concentrations (Tmax) approximately 80–105 minutes after oral administration [1][2]. Oral bioavailability of psilocin from oral psilocybin is approximately 52–77% [2][4]. Food has minimal effect on absorption rate but may delay Tmax slightly.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: Psilocin is widely distributed throughout the body and readily crosses the blood-brain barrier, consistent with its central psychoactive effects. Plasma protein binding is approximately 50%. The volume of distribution is large (Vd ~1.2–1.4 L/kg), reflecting extensive tissue penetration [1][2]. CNS distribution to cortical 5-HT2A receptors is the pharmacologically relevant compartment.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: Psilocybin is rapidly converted to psilocin (active form) via alkaline phosphatase-mediated dephosphorylation. Psilocin is subsequently metabolised via two primary pathways: (1) monoamine oxidase (MAO)-mediated oxidative deamination to 4-hydroxyindole-3-acetic acid (4-HIAA), and (2) UGT1A10-mediated glucuronidation to psilocin-O-glucuronide [3]. Minor metabolites include 4-hydroxytryptophol and norpsilocin. Significant interindividual variability in metabolic rate has been documented across clinical studies [3][4].
pharmacokinetics.excretion: Primarily renal. Approximately 15–20% of the administered psilocybin dose is recovered in urine as unchanged psilocin; the remainder is excreted as glucuronide conjugates and 4-HIAA [3][4]. The terminal elimination half-life of psilocin is approximately 2–3 hours [1][2]. Excretion is essentially complete within 24 hours, with no evidence of accumulation.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Lithium carbonate — risk of seizures and hyperthermia (case reports of life-threatening interactions). MAOIs — significantly prolong and intensify effects; risk of serotonin syndrome. SSRIs — blunt acute psychedelic effects (5-HT2A downregulation); not thought to be dangerous but may reduce efficacy. Tramadol — serotonin syndrome risk.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: Physiological toxicity very low: LD50 estimated at 280 mg/kg (oral, rats) — far above any human dose. No organ toxicity documented. Psychological risk (acute anxiety, rare HPPD) is the dominant clinical risk. Psilocybin is physiologically non-addictive and produces rapid tolerance; formal long-term safety data are limited to early-phase clinical research.

special_precautions.pregnancy: No human data. Avoid in pregnancy. Animal studies show no teratogenicity at sub-psychedelic doses, but clinical use in pregnant individuals is contraindicated.
special_precautions.lactation: No human data on breast milk excretion. Contraindicated during breastfeeding.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: Hepatic glucuronidation is the primary metabolic pathway; significant hepatic impairment may increase psilocin exposure. No clinical dose-adjustment data available.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: Primary excretion is renal; renal impairment may prolong psilocin exposure. No clinical dose-adjustment data. Monitor carefully.

cited_references: [1] - Carhart-Harris R., "Trial of Psilocybin versus Escitalopram for Depression.", N Engl J Med, 2021, PMID: 33852780
[2] - Davis AK., "Effects of Psilocybin-Assisted Therapy on Major Depressive Disorder: A Randomized Clinical Trial.", JAMA Psychiatry, 2021, PMID: 33146667
[3] - Raison CL., "Single-Dose Psilocybin Treatment for Major Depressive Disorder: A Randomized Clinical Trial.", JAMA, 2023, PMID: 37651119
[4] - Rosenblat JD., "Psilocybin-assisted psychotherapy for treatment resistant depression: A randomized clinical trial.", Med, 2024, PMID: 38359838
[5] - Haikazian S., "Psilocybin-assisted therapy for depression: A systematic review and meta-analysis.", Psychiatry Res, 2023, PMID: 37844352

narrative_summary.historical_use: One of the most culturally iconic mushrooms globally. Used in shamanic traditions across Siberia and northern Europe for millennia as an entheogen. Historical use by the Koryak, Chukchi, and other Siberian shamans; speculated to be the 'Soma' of the Rigveda (Gordon Wasson's hypothesis, contested). The active compounds ibotenic acid and muscimol are entirely distinct from the psilocybin found in Psilocybe species. Culturally associated with Christmas/Yule traditions in northern Europe.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Interest is primarily pharmacological and research-oriented. Muscimol is a potent, selective GABA-A receptor agonist and is reviewed among classic neuroscience compounds [2]. Chemistry, biology, toxicology, and ethnomycology have been comprehensively reviewed [1]. Ibotenic acid is a potent glutamate (NMDA/AMPA) receptor agonist used experimentally to create animal models of neurodegeneration [1]. Analytical characterisation of ibotenic acid, muscimol, and ergosterol content in A. muscaria hydrolysate preparations has been published [5]. Accidental poisoning cases are documented clinically [3]. Microdosing with A. muscaria extracts is a growing unregulated consumer trend studied via online community analysis [4]; clinical safety data are absent.
narrative_summary.side_effects: TOXIC if consumed without preparation: nausea, vomiting, hypersalivation, sweating, miosis (muscarine — minor component). CNS effects: sedation, dissociation, confusion, delirium, ataxia (muscimol/ibotenic acid). Rarely fatal in adults; potentially fatal in children and small adults. Symptoms: cholinergic + anticholinergic + CNS-depressant syndrome. Ibotenic acid is neurotoxic.
narrative_summary.contraindications: Raw or unprepared Amanita muscaria must not be consumed therapeutically. Ibotenic acid is neurotoxic. CNS depressants — additive effect. Not suitable for self-administration. All use must be under controlled research conditions.

clinical_data.used_part: Cap (pileus) — pharmacological isolation of muscimol and ibotenic acid
primary_active_compounds: Ibotenic acid (alpha-amino-3-hydroxy-5-isoxazole-acetic acid — excitatory neurotoxin, decarboxylates to muscimol)
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Muscimol is a highly potent, selective GABA-A receptor agonist (structural analogue of GABA), producing sedation, anxiolysis, and psychedelic effects. Ibotenic acid is an NMDA receptor and mGluR agonist (excitotoxic at high doses); rapidly decarboxylated in vivo to muscimol. Muscimol also acts at cerebellar GABA-B receptors. Muscarine (minor component) activates mAChR (muscarinic acetylcholine receptors), contributing to cholinergic toxidrome symptoms.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Muscimol and ibotenic acid well absorbed orally. Ibotenic acid crosses the blood-brain barrier actively. Muscimol crosses BBB via facilitated transport.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Muscimol is water-soluble with moderate CNS penetration via passive diffusion; distributes to GABAergic neurons in cortex, cerebellum, and limbic system. Ibotenic acid is rapidly decarboxylated to muscimol in vivo. Muscimol is excreted largely unchanged in urine (~80%). Plasma half-life of muscimol approximately 3–5 hours.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Ibotenic acid decarboxylated to muscimol (in vivo and during food preparation). Muscimol excreted largely unchanged (urinary excretion). Minimal hepatic metabolism.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Muscimol: primarily renal excretion unchanged (~20% of ingested dose recovered in urine — sufficient for psychoactive effects in individuals who consume recycled urine, a documented traditional practice in Siberian shamanism).

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: CNS depressants (benzodiazepines, alcohol, barbiturates, opioids) — markedly additive CNS depression. Anticholinergic drugs — interaction with muscarine-mediated cholinergic effects. GABAergic drugs — additive GABA-A modulation.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: Ibotenic acid: neurotoxic; hippocampal lesions demonstrated at high experimental doses. LD50 of ibotenic acid in rodents: ~38 mg/kg (IV), higher for oral. LD50 of muscimol: ~45 mg/kg (IV, rodents). Human fatalities from Amanita muscaria are rare (primarily in children or after massive ingestion) but documented. NOT safe for uncontrolled use.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Contraindicated. Ibotenic acid is neurotoxic. Potential teratogenicity not fully characterised but neurotoxic mechanism is deeply concerning.
special_precautions.lactation: Contraindicated. Muscimol excreted in urine; breast milk excretion not characterised but highly plausible given CNS penetration.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: Minimal hepatic metabolism — less relevant. CNS/renal effects predominate. Avoid entirely.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: Muscimol excreted renally; reduced clearance in renal impairment prolongs CNS toxicity. Avoid entirely.
