narrative_summary.historical_use: One of the oldest living tree species on Earth (>200 million years old), revered in China for over 5,000 years as 'yin xing' (silver apricot). Leaves and seeds used in Traditional Chinese Medicine for respiratory conditions, urinary dysfunction, and memory decline. Introduced to Western pharmacology in the 1960s; the standardised extract EGb 761 was developed by Willmar Schwabe GmbH and remains the most extensively studied phytomedicine in Europe.
narrative_summary.modern_application: EGb 761 (standardised to 24% flavonol glycosides and 6% terpene lactones) is approved in Germany for symptomatic treatment of dementia and peripheral arterial disease. A 2025 meta-analysis of pooled RCT data demonstrates statistically significant benefits of EGb 761 for cognition, activities of daily living, and clinical improvement in dementia patients with a history of cerebral infarction, with a safety profile comparable to placebo [10]. Network meta-analysis confirms Ginkgo biloba among the most effective plant compounds for learning, memory, and executive function in healthy older adults [8]. A 2026 randomised clinical trial evaluated Ginkgo biloba extract for prevention of posterior capsule opacification following cataract surgery, extending its evidence base beyond neurological applications [7].
narrative_summary.side_effects: GI upset (nausea, diarrhoea), headache, dizziness, and palpitations are common. Skin hypersensitivity reactions are possible. Rarely, spontaneous bleeding events including subdural haematoma and vitreous haemorrhage have been reported in case series. Ginkgolic acids in crude preparations are nephrotoxic and potentially carcinogenic; pharmaceutical extracts limit these to <5 ppm. Herb-drug interactions at the CYP450 level can precipitate adverse drug reactions through altered drug plasma levels [11].
narrative_summary.contraindications: Contraindicated with anticoagulants (warfarin, heparin) and antiplatelet drugs (aspirin, clopidogrel, NSAIDs) due to documented INR alteration and additive bleeding risk [4, 13]. Discontinue at least 36 hours before surgery. Caution with antiepileptics metabolised via CYP2C9, including phenytoin [6]. Avoid in patients with a history of seizures. Avoid unpurified leaf preparations containing elevated ginkgolic acids.

clinical_data.used_part: Leaf (standardised extract EGb 761); seed (limited therapeutic use — nephrotoxic in raw form)
primary_active_compounds: Ginkgo flavonol glycosides (quercetin-3-rutinoside, kaempferol-3-rutinoside, isorhamnetin glycosides)
Ginkgolides A, B, C, J (diterpene lactones)
Ginkgolic acids (alkyl/alkenyl phenols — limited to <5 ppm in EGb 761)
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Ginkgo biloba enhances cerebral perfusion and facilitates synaptic plasticity through convergent monoaminergic and neurotrophic pathways, with documented inhibitory activity at monoamine oxidase A (MAO-A) and MAO-B [3]. The biflavonoid ginkgetin exerts pleiotropic anti-inflammatory activity through inhibition of NF-κB and suppression of pro-inflammatory cytokines, alongside antioxidant and antifibrotic effects via multi-target signalling modulation [9]. Ginkgolide B acts as a selective antagonist at the platelet-activating factor (PAF) receptor, inhibiting platelet aggregation. Flavonol glycosides scavenge reactive oxygen species and upregulate Nrf2/HO-1 antioxidant defence genes, protecting against oxidative neuronal damage [3]. The extract modulates CYP1A2, CYP2C9, CYP2D6, and CYP3A4 activity, with clinically relevant implications for co-administered drugs [1].

pharmacokinetics.absorption: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Preclinical studies of Ginkgo biloba semen-derived components (GK-A, GK-2, Eucomic acid) in rats demonstrate rapid gastrointestinal absorption following oral administration [5]. Flavonol glycosides are hydrolysed in the gut by intestinal beta-glucosidases to their aglycones (quercetin, kaempferol, isorhamnetin) prior to systemic absorption; this biotransformation step is modulated by the intestinal microbiome and may affect bioavailability in dysbiotic states [2].
pharmacokinetics.distribution: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Rat pharmacokinetic studies of Ginkgo semen extract components demonstrate wide tissue distribution following oral dosing, with detection in brain, liver, kidney, and plasma [5]. Lipophilic terpene lactones are predicted to cross the blood-brain barrier based on animal tissue distribution data, consistent with the clinically observed central nervous system effects of EGb 761 [3].
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Rat liver microsomal studies of Ginkgo semen extract components indicate slow hepatic biotransformation, with CYP450 enzymes contributing only minimally as primary metabolising enzymes for the major active constituents [5]. However, at the whole-extract level, Ginkgo biloba modulates CYP1A2, CYP2C9, CYP2D6, and CYP3A4 activity in a clinically significant manner [1, 2], with the potential to alter plasma levels of co-administered substrates.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Preclinical pharmacokinetic profiling of Ginkgo semen components in rats identified renal excretion as the primary elimination route, with elimination kinetics characterised across major tissues [5]. Exact half-life values for the standardised leaf extract EGb 761 components in humans have not been captured in the peer-reviewed literature retrieved for this monograph.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Anticoagulants (warfarin): Ginkgo biloba is documented to alter INR in patients on warfarin, constituting a clinically significant interaction requiring vigilant therapeutic monitoring [4]. Antiepileptics (phenytoin): A 2025 systematic review identified Ginkgo biloba as a clinically relevant interaction partner for phenytoin, with CYP2C9 modulation as the primary mechanism [6]. CYP450 substrates (CYP1A2, CYP2C9, CYP2D6, CYP3A4): Ginkgo biloba significantly modulates all four major CYP isoforms, affecting pharmacokinetics of co-administered drugs across multiple therapeutic classes [1, 2]. Antithrombotic agents (aspirin, clopidogrel, NSAIDs): Additive antiplatelet activity documented, with increased bleeding risk when combined with antithrombotic therapies [13]. CYP450-mediated herb-drug interactions can precipitate adverse events through toxic elevation of drug plasma concentrations [11].
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: Ginkgolic acids are nephrotoxic and genotoxic at elevated concentrations; pharmaceutical EGb 761 extract limits these to <5 ppm. Ginkgotoxin (4'-O-methylpyridoxine) present in seeds is seizurogenic and pyridoxine-responsive; leaf extracts contain minimal amounts. Ginkgetin demonstrates a broad preclinical safety profile across anti-inflammatory and antifibrotic applications [9]. High-dose chronic rodent studies (NTP) identified hepatocellular adenoma; clinical relevance in humans at standard therapeutic doses remains uncertain.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Insufficient human safety data available. Ginkgolic acids are potentially teratogenic in animal models. Avoid during pregnancy.
special_precautions.lactation: Excretion into breast milk is unknown. Avoid due to insufficient safety data.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: CYP1A2, CYP2C9, CYP2D6, and CYP3A4 are hepatically expressed; impaired hepatic metabolism is expected in liver disease, increasing risk of drug interactions [1, 2]. No formal dose adjustment studies have been conducted. Use with caution.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: Renal excretion is the primary elimination route for active metabolites based on preclinical data [5]. Accumulation of active constituents is possible in severe renal impairment. No formal dose adjustment guidelines are established.

consumer_view.tagline: Sharpens memory and improves blood flow to the brain and limbs
consumer_view.what_it_does: Ginkgo increases blood flow to the brain, protects nerve cells from damage, and prevents blood from clumping in small vessels. Most people use it to support memory, concentration, and mental sharpness — especially with age. It is also widely used for tinnitus and cold hands and feet.
typical_uses: Poor circulation in hands and feet
consumer_view.suggested_dose: 120-240 mg of standardised extract (EGb 761; 24% flavonol glycosides, 6% terpene lactones) per day, split into 2 doses with meals. Allow 4-8 weeks for cognitive effects.
consumer_view.onset: Circulation and alertness: 1-2 weeks. Memory and cognitive benefits: 4-8 weeks of consistent use.
safety_snapshot: Do not take with blood thinners (warfarin, aspirin, clopidogrel)
Stop at least 36 hours before any surgery
Avoid if you have a history of seizures or are taking MAO inhibitors

narrative_summary.historical_use: Panax ginseng has been used in Traditional Chinese Medicine for over 2,000 years as a premier tonic herb ('ren shen' — man root), prescribed to restore vital energy (qi), strengthen the immune system, and extend longevity. Historical preparations included decoctions of dried root used for fatigue, cognitive decline, sexual dysfunction, and recovery from illness. Korean red ginseng — produced by steaming and drying — has been a distinct preparation valued for its enhanced ginsenoside profile and preserved since the Joseon dynasty.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Panax ginseng is among the world's most extensively studied adaptogens, with clinical evidence supporting its use in fatigue management, cognitive enhancement, metabolic regulation, and mood support. A 2026 randomised double-blind clinical trial demonstrated that 250 mg/day of ginseng extract significantly reduced cancer-related fatigue in gastrointestinal cancer survivors with excellent tolerability [3]. A 2026 randomised controlled crossover study found that ginseng consumed 90 minutes before a meal reduced postprandial glucose by 29% and insulin responses by 24% in healthy middle-aged adults [7]. Ginsenoside Rg1 shows multi-targeted pharmacological activity in neurodegenerative and metabolic disorders [6].
narrative_summary.side_effects: Generally well tolerated at standard doses; no adverse events were reported in the 2026 cancer-fatigue RCT [3]. Common side effects at higher doses include insomnia, headache, agitation, GI upset, and hypertension ('ginseng abuse syndrome'). Hepatotoxicity has been documented with prolonged or high-dose use [5]. Oestrogenic effects reported in some case series; mastalgia and vaginal bleeding possible in postmenopausal women. Hypoglycaemia risk is clinically relevant given documented glucose-lowering effects [7].
narrative_summary.contraindications: Contraindicated with warfarin due to CYP2C9 interaction and antiplatelet activity. Avoid with MAO inhibitors due to stimulant and monoaminergic properties [2]. Caution in patients on antidiabetic medications due to additive glucose-lowering effects [7]. Avoid in hormonally sensitive conditions (oestrogen receptor-positive cancers). Use with caution in patients with hepatic impairment given documented hepatotoxicity risk [5]. Caution with statins and other CYP3A4 substrates [8].

clinical_data.used_part: Root (main and lateral rootlets); Korean red ginseng (steamed and dried root)
primary_active_compounds: Compound K (active gut metabolite of protopanaxadiol ginsenosides)
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Ginsenoside Rg1 exerts pleiotropic pharmacological effects through simultaneous modulation of NF-κB, MAPK, and Wnt/β-catenin signalling pathways, conferring anti-inflammatory, neuroprotective, and antifibrotic activity [6]. Panax ginseng modulates monoaminergic neurotransmission — including dopaminergic, serotonergic, and noradrenergic systems — and suppresses neuroinflammation and oxidative stress, providing a mechanistic basis for antidepressant and cognitive-enhancing effects [2]. Ginsenosides inhibit alpha-glucosidase and modulate GLUT4 translocation, contributing to clinically documented postprandial glucose reduction [7]. The ginsenoside metabolite targeting PRELID3B demonstrates selective anticancer activity in lung cancer cell lines [9]. Panax ginseng also modulates CYP1A2, CYP2C9, CYP2D6, and CYP3A4 enzyme activity, with implications for co-administered drug metabolism [1].

pharmacokinetics.absorption: Oral bioavailability of native ginsenosides is inherently low and highly variable due to poor intestinal membrane permeability and extensive pre-systemic metabolism [4]. Nano-formulation strategies have been developed specifically to overcome these bioavailability limitations and improve systemic circulation of ginsenoside compounds [4]. Ginsenoside Rg1 in particular demonstrates significant bioavailability limitations following standard oral dosing, which constrains therapeutic plasma concentrations [6]. Timing of administration relative to meals is clinically relevant: a 2026 RCT showed that a 90-minute preload produced significant glucose and insulin effects absent when the same dose was taken immediately before eating [7].
pharmacokinetics.distribution: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Ginsenosides undergo extensive biotransformation by intestinal microbiota prior to absorption; the major active systemic metabolite in humans is Compound K (20-O-beta-(D-glucopyranosyl)-20(S)-protopanaxadiol), generated from protopanaxadiol-type ginsenosides. Protein binding and volume of distribution in humans have not been formally characterised for the major ginsenoside fractions.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Ginsenosides are primarily metabolised by intestinal bacterial enzymes (beta-glucosidases) to yield pharmacologically active aglycones and Compound K. Hepatic CYP450 enzymes — particularly CYP3A4 — contribute to further Phase I metabolism [1, 8]. Panax ginseng both inhibits and induces CYP450 isoforms (CYP1A2, CYP2C9, CYP2D6, CYP3A4) in a concentration- and constituent-dependent manner, introducing clinically significant variability in drug co-administration [1].
pharmacokinetics.excretion: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Ginsenoside metabolites are eliminated primarily via renal and faecal routes following hepatic conjugation. Half-life values vary substantially across individual ginsenosides and have not been systematically characterised in a standardised human pharmacokinetic study available in the retrieved literature.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: CYP450 substrates (CYP1A2, CYP2C9, CYP2D6, CYP3A4): Panax ginseng modulates all four major CYP isoforms, with potential to raise or lower plasma levels of co-administered drugs depending on isoform and ginseng preparation [1]. Statins and antidiabetic drugs (CYP3A4 pathway): Ginseng-mediated CYP3A4 modulation can alter the pharmacokinetics of atorvastatin, simvastatin, and oral antidiabetic agents; monitoring is warranted [8]. Antidiabetic agents (insulin, sulfonylureas, metformin): Clinically documented additive glucose-lowering effect — 29% postprandial glucose reduction in RCT [7] — creates significant hypoglycaemia risk requiring dose adjustment. Antidepressants (SSRIs, SNRIs, MAO inhibitors): Potential pharmacodynamic interactions through shared monoaminergic mechanisms; avoid with MAO inhibitors [2]. Hepatotoxic medications: Additive hepatotoxicity risk documented with prolonged high-dose use of Panax ginseng; avoid combination with known hepatotoxins [5].
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: Generally well tolerated at therapeutic doses (250 mg/day) with no adverse events in a 2026 cancer population RCT [3]. Hepatotoxicity is documented with high-dose or prolonged use and constitutes the primary safety concern [5]. 'Ginseng abuse syndrome' (hypertension, insomnia, nervousness, morning diarrhoea) is reported with doses exceeding 3 g/day of crude root. Oestrogenic activity at high doses may precipitate hormonal adverse effects. LD50 data for standardised extracts in humans are not established.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Insufficient human safety data. Animal studies raise concern for embryotoxicity at high doses. Avoid during pregnancy, particularly during the first trimester.
special_precautions.lactation: Ginsenoside excretion into breast milk has not been characterised in humans. Avoid during lactation due to insufficient safety data.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: Hepatotoxicity risk is documented with high-dose use [5]; CYP450-mediated metabolism is hepatically dependent [1]. Use with caution in patients with pre-existing hepatic impairment; liver function monitoring is advisable.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: Ginsenoside metabolites are renally eliminated. Accumulation is possible in severe renal impairment. No formal dose adjustment guidelines are established; use caution and monitor.

consumer_view.tagline: Boosts energy, focus, and resilience to stress
consumer_view.what_it_does: Panax ginseng is one of the most studied adaptogens in the world. It helps the body handle physical and mental stress more effectively, supports steady energy without the crash of stimulants, and has been shown in clinical trials to reduce fatigue and improve blood sugar control after meals.
consumer_view.suggested_dose: 200-400 mg/day of standardised extract (4-7% ginsenosides) or 250 mg/day of root extract as used in RCTs. Take in the morning or early afternoon — avoid evening use due to stimulant effects. Cycle 3 months on, 2-4 weeks off.
consumer_view.onset: Energy and mood: 1-2 weeks. Blood sugar and cognitive effects: 4-8 weeks of consistent use.
safety_snapshot: Avoid with blood thinners (warfarin) and MAO inhibitors
Monitor blood sugar closely if taking diabetes medication
Do not combine with other stimulants or hepatotoxic substances

narrative_summary.historical_use: Bacopa monnieri (Brahmi) has been used in Ayurvedic medicine for over 3,000 years, classified as a 'medhya rasayana' — a herb that rejuvenates the intellect and memory. It was historically prescribed for epilepsy, insanity, and cognitive decline, and documented in ancient Sanskrit texts including the Charaka Samhita. Its Sanskrit name 'Brahmi' honours Brahma, the creator deity, reflecting its revered status as a brain tonic.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Bacopa monnieri is extensively studied as a nootropic for cognitive enhancement and neuroprotection. A 2026 double-blind, placebo-controlled RCT protocol evaluates its effects on aging and neurocognitive function including telomere length in healthy adults [1]. Network meta-analysis of randomised controlled trials identifies Bacopa monnieri among the most effective plant-derived cognitive enhancers in older adults [2]. A systematic review confirms consistent improvement in inattention, hyperactivity, and memory in ADHD populations [6]. A 2026 double-blind RCT protocol further investigates Bacopa in amnestic mild cognitive impairment and early Alzheimer's disease [8], and its mechanisms in Alzheimer's neuropathology have been reviewed in depth [5].
narrative_summary.side_effects: Most commonly reported: nausea, increased intestinal motility, diarrhoea, and abdominal cramps — predominantly at the start of treatment and dose-dependent. Dry mouth and fatigue reported less frequently. GI side effects are significantly reduced when taken with food. Sedation is possible at high doses. Preclinical data demonstrate hepatorenal protective rather than toxic effects at therapeutic doses [10].
narrative_summary.contraindications: Avoid during pregnancy and lactation due to insufficient safety data. Caution in patients with bradycardia, thyroid conditions, or those taking thyroid medication, as cholinergic activity may slow heart rate. Avoid concurrent use with anticholinergic medications due to opposing mechanisms. Caution in patients with active GI conditions (ulcers, colitis) given its intestinal motility-enhancing effects.

clinical_data.used_part: Whole aerial parts (stem and leaves); standardised extracts containing 20-55% bacosides
primary_active_compounds: Bacoside A (mixture of bacoside A3, bacopasaponin C, and jujubogenin isomers)
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Bacosides A and B enhance synaptic transmission by inhibiting acetylcholinesterase (AChE) and upregulating choline acetyltransferase (ChAT), increasing central cholinergic tone to support memory consolidation and learning [5]. Bacopa monnieri exerts neuroprotection through suppression of NF-κB-mediated neuroinflammation and induction of antioxidant defence genes, reducing amyloid-beta aggregation and tau hyperphosphorylation in preclinical Alzheimer's models [5]. Bacoside-mediated neurostimulation enhances synaptic plasticity through upregulation of BDNF (brain-derived neurotrophic factor) and promotes dendritic arborisation in hippocampal neurons [3]. Apoptotic anticancer activity has been demonstrated via activation of the intrinsic mitochondrial apoptosis pathway (Bax/Bcl-2 ratio shift, cytochrome c release, caspase-3 activation) in lung cancer cell lines [9]. Bacopa also modulates dopaminergic and serotonergic neurotransmission, contributing to anxiolytic and antidepressant effects [7].

pharmacokinetics.absorption: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Oral bioavailability of bacosides is limited by poor aqueous solubility and first-pass hepatic metabolism; ongoing research specifically focuses on improving bioavailability through novel delivery systems [7]. Administration with food is recommended clinically to reduce GI side effects and may simultaneously improve absorption by slowing gastric transit.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Bacosides are lipid-soluble saponins capable of crossing the blood-brain barrier in animal models, consistent with the observed central neuroactive effects [3]. Tissue distribution in humans has not been formally characterised.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Bacosides undergo hepatic phase I and phase II biotransformation; the specific CYP450 isoforms involved in bacoside metabolism have not been systematically characterised in published human studies. Gut microbiota may contribute to pre-absorptive hydrolysis of saponin glycosides [7].
pharmacokinetics.excretion: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Excretion routes and elimination half-life for bacosides in humans have not been established in the peer-reviewed literature retrieved for this monograph. Renal and biliary excretion of conjugated metabolites is assumed based on structural analogy with related saponins.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Lack of clinical studies prevents interaction grading. Unknown safety profile with concomitant drug use.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: Bacopa monnieri demonstrates a favourable preclinical tolerability profile at therapeutic doses. Rat studies demonstrate hepatorenal protective effects against cisplatin-induced oxidative toxicity, with Bacopa extract reducing Kidney Injury Molecule-1 (KIM-1) levels and preserving hepatic architecture [10]. GI tolerability is dose-dependent; nausea and diarrhoea are the most consistently reported adverse effects in clinical trials, resolving with dose reduction or administration with food [7]. No serious adverse events were reported in ADHD supplement trials at standard doses [6]. Long-term human data beyond 12 weeks are not available from published clinical trials; extended-use recommendations cannot be made based on current evidence.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Insufficient human safety data available. Uterotonic effects reported in animal studies. Avoid during pregnancy.
special_precautions.lactation: Bacoside excretion into breast milk has not been studied. Medicinal-dose use during lactation is not recommended due to insufficient clinical safety data.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: Hepatic biotransformation of bacosides is expected to be impaired in liver disease [7]. Preclinical data show hepatoprotective properties at therapeutic doses [10]; however, altered metabolism may lead to unpredictable plasma levels. Use with caution.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: Preclinical evidence demonstrates renoprotective effects at therapeutic doses [10]. No formal human pharmacokinetic or dose adjustment studies are available. Use with caution in severe renal impairment.

consumer_view.tagline: Strengthens memory and calms the mind
consumer_view.what_it_does: Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) is one of Ayurveda's most celebrated brain herbs. It works by supporting the brain chemicals involved in memory and learning, while reducing inflammation in the nervous system. Clinical studies show it consistently improves attention, memory, and focus — especially with regular use over 8-12 weeks.
consumer_view.suggested_dose: 300-450 mg/day of standardised extract (20-55% bacosides). Always take with food to minimise GI side effects. Allow 8-12 weeks for full cognitive benefits — effects are cumulative, not immediate.
consumer_view.onset: GI side effects resolve within 1-2 weeks. Cognitive improvements: 8-12 weeks of consistent daily use.
safety_snapshot: Always take with food — reduces nausea and stomach upset
Avoid during pregnancy
Caution if taking anticholinergic medications or thyroid drugs

narrative_summary.historical_use: Rhodiola rosea has been used for centuries in traditional medicine across Siberia, Scandinavia, and the Arctic regions, where it was prescribed to enhance physical endurance, resistance to high-altitude conditions, and mental stamina. Viking warriors reportedly used it to enhance strength and endurance. Soviet military and athletic programmes investigated it extensively from the 1940s onward as a performance-enhancing adaptogen.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Rhodiola rosea is supported by converging evidence for antidepressant, anti-fatigue, and neuroprotective effects. A 2026 review identifies Rhodiola rosea as elevating monoamine concentrations and modulating pathways involved in the stress response, placing it alongside Ginkgo and Ashwagandha as a clinically relevant antidepressant supplement [1]. Clinical and preclinical data support its use for major depressive disorder through multi-targeted monoaminergic and anti-inflammatory mechanisms [2]. A 2025 randomised study demonstrates that Rhodiola rosea supplementation combined with eccentric training produces additive antifatigue and muscle-protective effects, improving endurance performance [6]. Salidroside, its primary bioactive, is under investigation for rheumatoid arthritis due to antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties [7].
narrative_summary.side_effects: Generally well tolerated at recommended doses. The most commonly reported side effects include dry mouth, dizziness, agitation, and insomnia when taken late in the day. Stimulant-like effects may exacerbate anxiety at high doses. Product quality is highly variable: rosavin — a key marker compound — was detected in only 9 of 13 commercially tested Rhodiola products, highlighting significant quality control concerns [4].
narrative_summary.contraindications: Avoid concurrent use with MAO inhibitors due to additive monoaminergic stimulation [1, 2]. Caution with antidepressants (SSRIs, SNRIs) given pharmacodynamic overlap. Avoid in bipolar disorder (potential for hypomanic switch). Use caution in patients on CYP450-metabolised drugs given salidroside's documented effect on human CYP450 activity [3]. Avoid in pregnancy and lactation due to insufficient safety data.

clinical_data.used_part: Root and rhizome (standardised extract; markers: rosavins ≥3%, salidroside ≥1%)
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Rhodiola rosea exerts adaptogenic effects primarily through elevation of monoamine neurotransmitter concentrations — serotonin, dopamine, and norepinephrine — and modulation of the HPA (hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal) axis stress response [1]. Salidroside demonstrates potent antioxidant activity via upregulation of Nrf2/HO-1 pathway, and anti-inflammatory effects through inhibition of NF-κB and reduction of TNF-α and IL-6 cytokine production, with therapeutic implications for rheumatoid arthritis [7]. The extract modulates HIF-1α (hypoxia-inducible factor 1-alpha), contributing to anti-hypoxic and altitude adaptation properties identified across Rhodiola species [4]. Salidroside also inhibits monoamine oxidase A (MAO-A) and MAO-B, potentiating monoaminergic neurotransmission and contributing to antidepressant and anti-fatigue effects [1, 2].

pharmacokinetics.absorption: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Oral bioavailability of rosavins and salidroside is limited by aqueous solubility constraints; cyclodextrin/PVP nanofiber formulations have been developed specifically to improve delivery and bioavailability of Rhodiola bioactives [5]. Product variability is clinically significant: rosavin content was undetectable in 4 of 13 commercially available products tested [4], meaning inter-product PK differences may be substantial.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Salidroside and rosavins are distributed to target tissues including the CNS and musculoskeletal system based on preclinical pharmacodynamic evidence and observed anti-fatigue effects in clinical exercise studies [6]. Precise volume of distribution and protein binding in humans have not been characterised.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — A 2026 in vitro study directly investigated the effect of salidroside on metabolic activity of both rat and human cytochrome P450 enzymes, demonstrating measurable modulation of CYP450 activity [3]. The specific isoforms and direction of effect (inhibition vs. induction) and clinical magnitude require interpretation from the primary study data.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Elimination pathways and half-life for salidroside and rosavins in humans have not been formally established in published clinical pharmacokinetic studies. Renal excretion of phase II conjugates is assumed based on structural analogy with related phenylpropanoid glycosides.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: CYP450 substrates: Salidroside directly modulates metabolic activity of human and rat cytochrome P450 enzymes in vitro [3]; patients co-administering drugs with narrow therapeutic indices metabolised by CYP450 should be monitored. Antidepressants (MAO inhibitors, SSRIs, SNRIs): Pharmacodynamic interaction risk through shared monoaminergic mechanism; combination with MAO inhibitors is contraindicated due to additive monoamine elevation [1, 2]. Herb-drug interactions pose documented risks in the context of complex pharmacokinetics [2]; full interaction profile has not been characterised clinically.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: Rhodiola rosea has a favourable preclinical and clinical safety profile at standardised doses. No serious adverse events are reported in clinical adaptogen trials. The primary safety concern is stimulant-like CNS excitation at high doses (agitation, insomnia). Product adulteration and substitution are documented risks given that rosavin content is absent in a significant proportion of commercial products [4]. LD50 data in humans are not established.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Insufficient human safety data. Avoid during pregnancy due to potential stimulant and HPA-modulating effects.
special_precautions.lactation: Excretion into breast milk has not been studied. Avoid during lactation.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: CYP450-dependent metabolism expected to be altered in hepatic disease; salidroside modulates CYP450 activity [3]. Use with caution and monitor for altered drug levels of co-administered medications.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: No formal studies in renal impairment. Renal excretion of conjugated metabolites is likely; use caution in severe renal impairment.

consumer_view.tagline: Fights fatigue and lifts mood under stress
consumer_view.what_it_does: Rhodiola is an Arctic adaptogen that helps your body cope with physical and mental stress. It works by balancing stress hormones and brain chemicals involved in mood and energy. Studies show it reduces fatigue, improves endurance, and has antidepressant effects comparable to some medications — with a gentler side effect profile.
consumer_view.suggested_dose: 300-600 mg/day of standardised extract (3% rosavins, 1% salidroside). Take in the morning or before exercise — avoid evening use. Cycle 3 months on, 2-4 weeks off.
consumer_view.onset: Energy and mood: 1-2 weeks. Anti-fatigue and endurance effects: 2-4 weeks.
safety_snapshot: Do not combine with antidepressants or MAO inhibitors without medical supervision
Take in the morning — can cause insomnia if taken late
Check product quality: many commercial products lack adequate rosavin content

narrative_summary.historical_use: Withania somnifera (Ashwagandha) has been a cornerstone of Ayurvedic medicine for over 3,000 years, classified as a 'rasayana' (rejuvenating tonic) and 'balya' (strength-promoting). Its Latin species name 'somnifera' (sleep-inducing) reflects its traditional use as a sedative and anxiolytic. Historical texts describe its use for debility, emaciation, nervous exhaustion, and as an aphrodisiac. The Sanskrit name 'Ashwagandha' (smell of horse) refers both to the root's odour and the traditional belief that it confers equine strength.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Ashwagandha is one of the most clinically investigated adaptogens in modern phytomedicine. A 2026 randomised trial demonstrates significant reductions in anxiety scores and cortisol levels in patients with stress, anxiety, and hypertension [3]. An 8-week RCT confirms improvements in VO2max and reduced exercise-induced fatigue at low doses [4], and a 2026 study shows improvements in quality of life and sleep parameters in elderly adults [5]. Ashwagandha modulates the HPA and HPG axes with documented relevance for PCOS and PMS through hormonal normalisation [6]. Its multi-targeted monoaminergic and neurotrophic mechanisms support a role as a novel antidepressant supplement [9].
narrative_summary.side_effects: A 2026 critical review of preclinical and clinical evidence documents gastrointestinal effects (nausea, diarrhoea), sedation, and hepatic adverse effects as primary adverse effects of clinical concern [7]. Thyroid hormone elevation has been reported in susceptible individuals, particularly those with pre-existing thyroid dysfunction [8]. Sedation and drowsiness are expected at higher doses given GABAergic mechanisms. Rare but serious adverse events include drug-induced liver injury (DILI) — multiple case reports of hepatotoxicity requiring medical attention have been documented [7]. Long-term safety data beyond 12 weeks are limited.
narrative_summary.contraindications: Contraindicated in patients with autoimmune disease (systemic lupus erythematosus, rheumatoid arthritis, multiple sclerosis) due to potential immune stimulation. Contraindicated in thyroid disease or concurrent thyroid medication use given documented thyroid hormone modulation [8]. Avoid with barbiturates, benzodiazepines, and other CNS depressants due to additive sedation [2]. Caution with immunosuppressants. Contraindicated in pregnancy (traditional abortifacient). Caution with CYP450-metabolised drugs [2].

clinical_data.used_part: Root (primary); leaf extract (secondary, higher withanolide concentration)
primary_active_compounds: Iron and amino acids (free radical scavengers)
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Ashwagandha exerts adaptogenic effects primarily through modulation of the HPA (hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal) axis, reducing serum cortisol levels and blunting the stress response — a mechanism confirmed in a 2026 clinical trial [3]. Withanolides bind to and modulate GABA-A receptors, producing anxiolytic and sedative effects without significant respiratory depression [2]. Multi-targeted antidepressant activity converges on serotonergic, dopaminergic, and noradrenergic neurotransmitter systems alongside suppression of NF-κB-driven neuroinflammation [9]. Withania somnifera modulates thyroid hormone synthesis — regulating T3 and T4 levels — through upregulation of thyroid-specific gene expression [8]. Immune-modulatory effects are mediated through Th1/Th2 balance and NK cell activation, relevant to oncological applications [1].

pharmacokinetics.absorption: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Oral bioavailability of withanolides, particularly Withaferin A, is limited by poor aqueous solubility and significant first-pass hepatic metabolism. Lipid-based formulations and black pepper (piperine) co-administration are used commercially to enhance absorption, though formal human bioavailability studies with standardised extracts are lacking.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Withanolides are lipophilic and distribute to CNS, adrenal, and reproductive tissues in animal models, consistent with the observed HPA axis modulation and hormonal effects [6]. Plasma protein binding and volume of distribution in humans have not been formally characterised.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Withania somnifera extracts interact with cytochrome P450 enzymes; a 2026 safety review specifically identifies CYP450 interactions as a documented concern for herb-drug interactions in clinical practice [2]. The specific isoforms involved and degree of inhibition or induction have not been characterised in standardised human in vivo studies.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Elimination routes and half-life of withanolides in humans are not established in published clinical pharmacokinetic studies. Phase II hepatic conjugation followed by renal excretion is expected based on structural characteristics of the steroidal lactone backbone.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: CNS depressants (benzodiazepines, barbiturates, opioids, alcohol): Additive sedation through shared GABAergic mechanism; combination requires caution and dose reduction of the depressant agent [2]. CYP450 substrates: Ashwagandha extracts are documented to interact with cytochrome P450 enzymes, potentially altering plasma levels of co-administered drugs metabolised by CYP pathways [2]. Thyroid medications (levothyroxine, antithyroid drugs): Withania somnifera modulates T3/T4 synthesis and gene expression [8]; combination with thyroid medications may produce unpredictable hormonal effects requiring monitoring. Immunosuppressants (tacrolimus, cyclosporine): Potential immune-stimulating activity may antagonise immunosuppressive therapy. Antihypertensives: Additive blood pressure-lowering effects observed in a 2026 RCT in hypertensive patients [3].
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: A 2026 comprehensive adverse effects review documents hepatotoxicity as the most clinically serious risk — multiple case reports of drug-induced liver injury (DILI) including acute hepatitis have been attributed to Ashwagandha, predominantly at high doses or with prolonged use [7]. GI effects (nausea, diarrhoea) are the most common adverse events in RCTs. Thyroid hormone elevation, including reports of hyperthyroidism, is a documented endocrine risk [7, 8]. LD50 data in humans are not established; standardised extract doses of 300-600 mg/day are generally well tolerated in short-term (8-12 week) clinical trials [3, 4].

special_precautions.pregnancy: Contraindicated. Ashwagandha is a traditional abortifacient; uterotonic alkaloids are present in the root. Avoid throughout pregnancy.
special_precautions.lactation: Withanolide excretion into breast milk has not been characterised. Medicinal-dose use during lactation is not recommended due to insufficient clinical safety data and the potential for hormonal effects from withanolide activity.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: Hepatotoxicity risk is documented [7]; CYP450-dependent metabolism is hepatically expressed [2]. Contraindicated in patients with active hepatic disease. Liver function monitoring is strongly advisable in all patients using Ashwagandha beyond 8 weeks.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: No formal studies in renal impairment. Conjugated withanolide metabolites are renally excreted. Use with caution in severe renal impairment and monitor.

consumer_view.tagline: Reduces stress hormones and builds resilience
consumer_view.what_it_does: Ashwagandha is Ayurveda's most celebrated stress herb. It works directly on your stress hormone system (cortisol), calming the adrenal glands and supporting GABA receptors in the brain. Clinical trials show it reduces anxiety, lowers cortisol, improves sleep, and boosts physical endurance — all backed by recent randomised studies.
consumer_view.suggested_dose: 300-600 mg/day of standardised root extract (5% withanolides). Take with food. Evening dosing preferred for sleep benefits. 8-12 week cycles recommended.
consumer_view.onset: Anxiety and cortisol reduction: 2-4 weeks. Sleep and endurance improvements: 4-8 weeks of consistent use.
safety_snapshot: Avoid if pregnant — traditional abortifacient
Do not combine with sedatives or thyroid medication without medical supervision
Stop immediately and consult a doctor if you notice yellowing of skin or eyes (jaundice)

narrative_summary.historical_use: Valeriana officinalis has been used as a sedative and anxiolytic since antiquity — Hippocrates described its properties and Galen prescribed it for insomnia. The name derives from the Latin 'valere' (to be well). Widely used throughout medieval Europe for nervous disorders, it was listed in the US Pharmacopoeia until 1942 and remains among the most commonly used herbal medicinal products in primary care globally [8].
narrative_summary.modern_application: Valeriana officinalis demonstrates evidence-based efficacy for insomnia and anxiety through multi-organ, multi-target mechanisms [1]. A 2026 comprehensive review confirms its role as a GABAergic, serotonergic, and adenosinergic modulator with documented clinical benefits for insomnia [5]. A randomised controlled trial demonstrated significant reduction in anxiety scores compared to placebo in patients undergoing tooth extraction [4]. Valerian is the most commonly cited herbal product in Brazilian primary care settings, though clinical users face risk of herb-drug interactions [8].
narrative_summary.side_effects: Generally well tolerated. Paradoxical stimulant effects reported in some individuals, particularly children. Morning grogginess ('hangover effect') at higher doses. Rare: hepatotoxicity in case reports. Potential for additive CNS depression with concomitant sedative agents [2]. Abrupt discontinuation after prolonged use may cause withdrawal symptoms analogous to benzodiazepine discontinuation.
narrative_summary.contraindications: Contraindicated with benzodiazepines, barbiturates, and opioids due to additive CNS depression [2]. Avoid with CYP3A4-metabolised drugs with narrow therapeutic indices — direct CYP3A inhibition and P-glycoprotein modulation documented [3]. Avoid with alcohol. Caution in patients with hepatic impairment. Do not use in pregnancy or lactation.

clinical_data.used_part: Root and rhizome (dried or as hydroethanolic extract)
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Valerenic acid and its derivatives are direct partial agonists at the GABA-A receptor (beta-subunit), potentiating inhibitory neurotransmission and producing anxiolytic and sedative effects without full benzodiazepine-receptor occupancy [1]. The extract modulates serotonin (5-HT5a) receptor binding and inhibits synaptosomal serotonin reuptake, contributing to mood-stabilising effects [6]. Valepotriates act as adenosine receptor ligands and modulate adenosine A1 receptors, promoting sleep through adenosinergic pathways [5]. Multi-organ actions include gastrointestinal spasmolysis via smooth muscle relaxation and autonomic nervous system dampening [1].

pharmacokinetics.absorption: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Valerenic acid is the primary monitored pharmacokinetic marker. Valepotriates are highly unstable and largely degrade in the GI tract prior to systemic absorption; their in vivo activity may be mediated by degradation products (baldrinals). Oral bioavailability of valerenic acid has not been formally characterised in a published human PK study.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Valerenic acid is lipophilic and expected to cross the blood-brain barrier, consistent with the observed central sedative and anxiolytic effects [1]. Protein binding and volume of distribution in humans are not established.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Valeriana officinalis extract inhibits CYP3A4 activity and modulates P-glycoprotein efflux transporter function, as demonstrated in direct experimental studies of intestinal metabolism of midazolam [3]. This interaction is clinically significant for drugs with high first-pass CYP3A4 metabolism.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Elimination routes and half-life of valerenic acid and active metabolites in humans are not established in published clinical pharmacokinetic studies.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: CYP3A4 substrates and P-glycoprotein substrates: Valeriana officinalis extracts directly inhibit intestinal CYP3A4 metabolism and modulate P-glycoprotein, demonstrated by inhibition of midazolam intestinal metabolism in vitro [3]; drugs with narrow therapeutic windows metabolised by CYP3A4 (e.g. cyclosporine, tacrolimus, statins, benzodiazepines) require monitoring. CNS depressants (benzodiazepines, barbiturates, opioids, alcohol): Additive sedation through shared GABAergic mechanism is documented; combination increases risk of excessive CNS depression [2]. Antidepressants: Complex pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic interactions possible through serotonergic and CYP450 pathways [6]. High potential for herb-drug interactions documented in primary care surveillance data [8].
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: Valeriana officinalis is well tolerated at standard doses in available clinical studies; adverse effects are generally mild. Rare case reports of hepatotoxicity exist, likely attributable to valepotriate alkylating activity at high doses. Paradoxical excitation reported particularly in paediatric populations [6]. No established LD50 in humans. Abrupt discontinuation after prolonged use may produce a withdrawal syndrome. Long-term use beyond 4–6 weeks has not been systematically evaluated in controlled trials; long-term safety data are accordingly limited.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Valepotriates are cytotoxic and potentially mutagenic in vitro. Avoid during pregnancy.
special_precautions.lactation: Excretion in breast milk unknown. Avoid during lactation due to insufficient safety data.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: Rare hepatotoxicity cases documented. CYP3A4 inhibition [3] will be amplified in hepatic impairment. Contraindicated in active hepatic disease.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: No formal studies in renal impairment. Use with caution; renal excretion of conjugated metabolites expected.

consumer_view.tagline: Natural sleep aid and nerve calmer
consumer_view.what_it_does: Valerian works like a gentle, natural version of anti-anxiety medication — it binds to the same brain receptors as benzodiazepines but more weakly, helping to calm an overactive nervous system and ease you into sleep. Clinical trials show it reduces anxiety in stressful situations and improves sleep quality.
consumer_view.suggested_dose: 300-600 mg of standardised root extract 30-60 minutes before bed. For anxiety: 120-200 mg up to 3x daily. Allow 2-4 weeks for full sleep benefits.
consumer_view.onset: Single-dose anxiolytic effect: 30-60 minutes. Sleep quality improvement: 2-4 weeks of regular use.
safety_snapshot: Do not combine with sleeping pills, benzodiazepines, or alcohol
Can cause morning drowsiness — avoid driving if affected
Do not take during pregnancy

narrative_summary.historical_use: Chamomile (Matricaria chamomilla) is one of the oldest and most widely used medicinal herbs in European traditional medicine, with documented use dating to ancient Egypt and classical antiquity. Hippocrates, Dioscorides, and Asclepius all described its therapeutic applications. It has been used across cultures for GI complaints, skin conditions, inflammation, anxiety, and insomnia, and it remains listed in the pharmacopoeias of 26 countries.
narrative_summary.modern_application: German chamomile holds a broad evidence base across multiple therapeutic domains. A triple-blind RCT demonstrates efficacy in managing menopausal symptoms, though use requires caution due to associated side effects warranting further optimisation [3]. A 2025 randomised double-blind controlled trial confirms topical chamomile oil as a safe adjunctive intervention for diabetic neuropathy, producing statistically significant improvements in neuropathy indices [4]. A clinical trial evaluating chamomile oral solution alongside paclitaxel chemotherapy found improved sleep quality in cancer patients [5]. Meta-analysis supports benefits in PCOS for clinical and hormonal parameters [6].
narrative_summary.side_effects: Generally well tolerated. Allergic reactions — including anaphylaxis — are the most clinically significant risk, particularly in individuals sensitised to Asteraceae/Compositae (ragweed, chrysanthemum, marigold). Contact dermatitis with topical formulations. GI upset at high oral doses. Rare: uterotonic effects. The 2025 menopausal trial noted associated side effects requiring further characterisation [3].
narrative_summary.contraindications: Contraindicated in patients with known allergy to Asteraceae plants. Avoid with warfarin — apigenin inhibits platelet aggregation and may potentiate anticoagulation. Caution with CYP3A4-metabolised drugs given documented CYP3A induction [1, 2]. Avoid in pregnancy due to potential uterotonic activity. Caution with CNS depressants due to additive sedation.

clinical_data.used_part: Dried flower heads; essential oil (steam-distilled); hydroethanolic extracts
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Apigenin, the principal flavone aglycone, is a partial agonist at GABA-A receptors (benzodiazepine-binding site), producing anxiolytic and mild sedative effects without significant tolerance development. Chamazulene and alpha-bisabolol potently inhibit COX-1, COX-2, and 5-lipoxygenase (5-LOX), suppressing prostaglandin and leukotriene synthesis and explaining the anti-inflammatory and spasmolytic effects [4]. Apigenin modulates aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) signalling, with emerging evidence for neuroinflammatory pathway modulation relevant to dementia therapy. Matricaria chamomilla extract induces hepatic CYP3A expression through upregulation of the circadian clock protein BMAL1 in liver nuclei [1], with potential consequences for drug metabolism timing and magnitude.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Apigenin-7-glucoside is hydrolysed by intestinal glucosidases to the aglycone apigenin prior to absorption. Apigenin bioavailability is limited by poor aqueous solubility; food co-ingestion and lipid-based formulations improve absorption. Topical chamomile oil shows significant transcutaneous absorption, producing measurable systemic exposure in clinical settings [4].
pharmacokinetics.distribution: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Apigenin is extensively protein-bound (>99%) in plasma and distributes to CNS, skin, and reproductive tissues based on pharmacodynamic evidence from clinical trials [3, 6]. The highly lipophilic chamazulene distributes readily to inflamed tissues.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — German chamomile induces hepatic CYP3A expression via the BMAL1 circadian mechanism, as directly demonstrated in liver nuclei [1]. This induction may reduce plasma levels of co-administered CYP3A4 substrates. Phase II metabolism involves glucuronidation and sulfation of apigenin aglycone by UGT and SULT enzymes.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Apigenin glucuronide and sulfate conjugates are excreted renally. Chamazulene and bisabolol are eliminated via renal and biliary routes. Elimination half-life of apigenin in humans has not been characterised in available literature.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: CYP3A4 substrates: German chamomile directly induces CYP3A expression in liver nuclei through a BMAL1-dependent mechanism [1], potentially lowering plasma levels of CYP3A4-metabolised drugs (statins, immunosuppressants, benzodiazepines, HIV antiretrovirals). The clinical magnitude is time-of-day dependent given the circadian regulation [1]. Anticoagulants (warfarin): Apigenin inhibits platelet aggregation; additive bleeding risk with anticoagulants requires monitoring [2]. Herb-drug interactions via CYP450 enzyme modulation are documented as a general class concern [2].
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: Anaphylaxis is the most serious documented adverse event; risk is highest in Asteraceae-allergic individuals. In the paclitaxel chemotherapy trial, chamomile was well tolerated and did not worsen neuropathy [5]. The menopausal clinical trial noted side effects requiring characterisation [3]. No hepatotoxicity reports at standard oral doses. Essential oil should not be used undiluted on skin due to risk of irritation. Oral essential oil ingestion is not recommended.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Potential uterotonic activity. Avoid oral chamomile preparations during pregnancy; topical use in limited areas is generally considered low-risk.
special_precautions.lactation: Insufficient human safety data. Small amounts excreted in breast milk possible. Use with caution.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: CYP3A induction [1] may be attenuated in hepatic impairment, reducing drug interaction risk but also reducing therapeutic metabolite production. Monitor accordingly.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: Flavonoid conjugates renally excreted. Accumulation possible in severe renal impairment; use with caution.

consumer_view.tagline: Gentle anti-inflammatory and stress reliever
consumer_view.what_it_does: Chamomile is one of the world's most-used herbs for good reason — it calms nerves, soothes inflammation, and relaxes muscle spasms. Its main compound apigenin works on the same brain receptors as anti-anxiety drugs, but much more gently. Clinically proven for menopausal symptoms, diabetic neuropathy relief, and sleep quality in cancer patients.
consumer_view.suggested_dose: Tea: 1-3 g dried flowers in 150 ml hot water, 3-4x daily. Extract: 220-1100 mg/day standardised to apigenin. Topical oil: dilute to 1-3% in carrier oil.
consumer_view.onset: Relaxation and GI relief: 30-60 minutes. Anti-inflammatory and hormonal effects: 4-8 weeks.
safety_snapshot: Avoid if allergic to ragweed, chrysanthemum, or marigold
Do not take with warfarin without medical supervision
May affect how quickly your liver breaks down medications

narrative_summary.historical_use: Passiflora incarnata (Maypop) is native to the southeastern United States and Central America, where it was used by indigenous peoples including the Cherokee and Aztecs as a sedative, analgesic, and antispasmodic. Spanish explorers introduced it to Europe in the 16th century; it was official in the US National Formulary from 1916 to 1936 and remains listed in the pharmacopoeias of France, Germany, and the UK.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Passionflower has a strong and growing clinical evidence base for anxiety and sleep disorders. A randomised double-blind placebo-controlled clinical trial demonstrates statistically significant reduction in stress and improvement in sleep quality with no adverse effects [8]. Multiple RCTs confirm anxiolytic efficacy in procedural anxiety during dental surgery [2, 4] — with performance comparable to traditional anxiolytics but fewer side effects [3]. Real-world clinical data support passionflower as an effective add-on during benzodiazepine tapering, with documented long-term safety and efficacy [6].
narrative_summary.side_effects: Generally well tolerated in clinical trials with no adverse events reported in the 2024 stress/sleep RCT [8]. Drowsiness and sedation are the most common dose-dependent effects. Rare: dizziness, confusion, vasculitis. Potential for serotonin syndrome when combined with serotonergic medications [1]. Risk of additive CNS depression with concomitant sedatives or alcohol.
narrative_summary.contraindications: Contraindicated with MAO inhibitors due to harman alkaloid content (MAO inhibition). Avoid with other CNS depressants without dose adjustment due to additive sedation [1]. Caution with anticoagulants — flavonoids may inhibit platelet aggregation. Avoid concurrent use with SSRIs/SNRIs due to serotonin syndrome risk [1]. Do not use during pregnancy (uterotonic alkaloids). Do not use before driving or operating machinery.

clinical_data.used_part: Aerial parts (leaves, stems, tendrils, flowers) — dried or as hydroethanolic extract
primary_active_compounds: Harman, harmol, harmalol (beta-carboline alkaloids — MAO inhibition)
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Chrysin, the principal flavone, binds to the benzodiazepine site of the GABA-A receptor, potentiating chloride ion influx and producing anxiolytic and sedative effects comparable to low-dose benzodiazepines [3]. The beta-carboline alkaloids (harman, harmol) reversibly inhibit monoamine oxidase A (MAO-A), elevating synaptic monoamine concentrations and contributing to antidepressant and anxiolytic effects [1]. Passiflora incarnata extract modulates cytochrome P450 enzyme activity, with documented risk of herb-drug interactions via this pathway [1]. Anti-inflammatory effects are mediated through inhibition of NF-κB and COX-2 pathways; hepatoprotective properties are documented in preclinical models [7].

pharmacokinetics.absorption: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Chrysin has poor oral bioavailability due to rapid phase II conjugation (glucuronidation and sulfation) and efflux by intestinal P-glycoprotein. C-glycosyl flavones (vitexin, isovitexin) require microbial deglycosylation in the large intestine prior to systemic absorption.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Chrysin and its metabolites distribute to CNS in preclinical models, consistent with the documented central anxiolytic and sedative effects [3, 8]. Protein binding of chrysin is high (>99%) in animal studies.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Chrysin undergoes rapid sulfation and glucuronidation by intestinal and hepatic phase II enzymes. Passiflora extracts interact with CYP450 enzymes with potential clinical consequences for co-administered drugs [1]. The beta-carboline alkaloids are substrates of MAO enzymes, contributing to mutual inhibition.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Chrysin conjugates are primarily renally excreted. Half-life and systemic elimination kinetics of the primary flavones in humans have not been characterised in formal pharmacokinetic studies.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: CNS depressants (benzodiazepines, opioids, alcohol): Additive sedation through shared GABAergic mechanism; the combination has been used clinically during benzodiazepine tapering without serious adverse events reported in the cited real-world study [6], but requires medical supervision. MAO inhibitors: Harman alkaloids inhibit MAO-A; combination with MAOI antidepressants is contraindicated due to risk of hypertensive crisis and serotonin syndrome [1]. SSRIs/SNRIs: Serotonin syndrome risk documented when combined with serotonergic agents [1]; monitor closely. CYP450 substrates: Herb-drug interactions via CYP450 modulation are a documented class risk [1].
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: No serious adverse events reported in multiple RCTs [8, 4]. The 2024 benzodiazepine tapering real-world study confirmed long-term safety over extended treatment periods [6]. Harman alkaloids in high doses carry theoretical MAO inhibition risk. No hepatotoxicity data in clinical use. LD50 in humans is not established.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Uterotonic alkaloids present. Contraindicated during pregnancy.
special_precautions.lactation: Alkaloid excretion into breast milk not characterised. Avoid during lactation.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: Hepatoprotective effects documented preclinically [7]. CYP450 interactions may be altered in hepatic disease [1]. Use with caution and monitor drug levels of co-administered medications.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: No formal studies. Flavonoid conjugates renally excreted. Use with caution in severe renal impairment.

consumer_view.tagline: Calms anxiety and improves sleep without dependence
consumer_view.what_it_does: Passionflower works on the same brain receptors as benzodiazepines (anti-anxiety drugs) but much more gently, reducing anxiety without the addiction risk. Clinical trials show it reduces procedural anxiety as effectively as some medications. It is also used clinically to help people taper off benzodiazepines more comfortably.
consumer_view.suggested_dose: 250-500 mg of standardised extract (3.5% isovitexin) or 1-2 g dried herb as tea. Take 30-60 minutes before bed for sleep, or as needed for anxiety.
consumer_view.onset: Acute anxiety relief: 30-60 minutes. Sleep improvement: 1-2 weeks of regular use.
safety_snapshot: Do not combine with MAO inhibitors — dangerous interaction
Avoid with alcohol or sleeping pills
Do not drive after taking — causes drowsiness

narrative_summary.historical_use: Nigella sativa (black seed) has been used medicinally for over 3,000 years across Islamic, Ayurvedic, and Egyptian traditional medicine. The Prophet Muhammad reportedly stated that black seed is 'a cure for every disease except death' (Sahih Al-Bukhari). Seeds were found in the tomb of Tutankhamun. Traditional uses span respiratory conditions, gastrointestinal ailments, diabetes, headache, and skin conditions.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Nigella sativa has a broad evidence base across metabolic, inflammatory, oncological, and infectious disease domains. An umbrella review of herbal medicines for glycaemic control confirms Nigella sativa improves insulin resistance with few reported adverse effects in type 2 diabetes [5]. A 2026 pilot clinical trial combining a Nigella sativa formulation (NP-101) with immune checkpoint inhibitors (nivolumab and ipilimumab) achieved a 41.7% objective response rate in treatment-resistant neuroendocrine cancers [2]. Systematic review of 15 paediatric RCTs found no statistically significant adverse effects [3]. Thymoquinone improves cognitive function in overweight individuals through antioxidant and anti-inflammatory molecular pathways [1].
narrative_summary.side_effects: Generally well tolerated in short-term clinical trials with no serious adverse effects in paediatric systematic review [3]. GI effects (nausea, bloating) at high doses. Topical oil may cause contact dermatitis. Thymoquinone at very high doses shows nephrotoxicity in animal models — not replicated at therapeutic human doses. Hypoglycaemia risk when combined with antidiabetic agents [5].
narrative_summary.contraindications: Caution with antidiabetic medications (insulin, metformin, sulfonylureas) due to documented additive glucose-lowering effect [5]. Caution with anticoagulants — thymoquinone inhibits platelet aggregation. Caution with immunosuppressants given immune-modulating properties. Avoid in pregnancy — uterotonic effects documented in animal models.

clinical_data.used_part: Seeds (whole, ground, or cold-pressed oil); standardised thymoquinone extracts
primary_active_compounds: Thymoquinone (30-48% of volatile oil — primary bioactive)
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Thymoquinone exerts neuroprotective effects in obese and overweight individuals through reduction of oxidative stress, suppression of NF-κB-mediated neuroinflammation, and modulation of AMPK and PI3K/Akt signalling pathways [1]. Antidiabetic activity is mediated through alpha-glucosidase inhibition, GLUT4 upregulation, and pancreatic beta-cell protection [4, 5]. Immunomodulatory properties include modulation of Th1/Th2 cytokine balance and NK cell activity, providing the rationale for combination with immune checkpoint inhibitors in cancer [2]. Anti-Helicobacter pylori activity is mediated through disruption of bacterial cell wall integrity and inhibition of urease enzyme [7]. Alpha-hederin demonstrates apoptotic activity through caspase-3 activation.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Thymoquinone has limited oral bioavailability due to poor aqueous solubility and rapid first-pass metabolism. Nanoparticle and nanoemulsion formulations have been developed to improve bioavailability, including in paediatric dentistry applications [3]. The fixed oil matrix in whole seed preparations may act as a natural lipid delivery vehicle, enhancing thymoquinone absorption.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Thymoquinone distributes to CNS, liver, kidney, and adipose tissue in animal models. CNS penetration is consistent with documented neuroprotective and cognitive effects in obese individuals [1].
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Thymoquinone is primarily metabolised by hepatic CYP450 enzymes; the specific isoforms involved and potential for drug interactions via CYP450 modulation have not been formally characterised in clinical studies.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Thymoquinone metabolites are excreted via renal and biliary routes in animal models. Elimination half-life in humans has not been established in published pharmacokinetic studies.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Lack of clinical studies prevents interaction grading. Unknown safety profile with concomitant drug use.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: Systematic review of 15 paediatric RCTs found no statistically significant adverse effects at therapeutic doses [3]. The 2026 clinical trial combining Nigella sativa with immunotherapy reported a manageable safety profile [2]. Thymoquinone shows dose-dependent nephrotoxicity in high-dose animal studies, not replicated at human therapeutic doses. Thymoquinone inhibits platelet aggregation and may increase bleeding risk. Standard whole seed doses (1–3 g/day) have demonstrated a favourable adverse effect profile in short-term adult clinical trials; long-term safety data are limited.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Uterotonic effects documented in animal studies. Avoid during pregnancy, particularly in the first trimester.
special_precautions.lactation: Thymoquinone excretion into breast milk not characterised in humans. Use with caution.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: CYP450-mediated hepatic metabolism expected; impaired clearance in liver disease. No formal dose adjustment data. Use with caution.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: High-dose thymoquinone nephrotoxicity in animal models warrants caution in renal impairment. No formal human studies. Monitor renal function.

consumer_view.tagline: Ancient black seed with powerful antioxidant and metabolic effects
consumer_view.what_it_does: Black seed (Nigella sativa) contains thymoquinone, one of the most studied natural anti-inflammatory compounds. Clinical evidence supports its use for blood sugar control in type 2 diabetes, immune support, and brain protection in overweight individuals. A recent clinical trial even tested it alongside cancer immunotherapy with promising results.
consumer_view.suggested_dose: 1-3 g/day of ground seed or 0.5-1 ml of cold-pressed oil. Take with meals. Standardised extracts: 300-500 mg thymoquinone-rich extract daily.
consumer_view.onset: Metabolic effects (blood sugar): 4-8 weeks. Anti-inflammatory and cognitive effects: 8-12 weeks.
safety_snapshot: Monitor blood sugar closely if on diabetes medication — additive lowering effect
Avoid during pregnancy
Speak to your doctor before combining with cancer treatments or blood thinners
