narrative_summary.historical_use: Urtica dioica (stinging nettle) has been used medicinally across Europe, North America, and Asia for at least 2,000 years. Hippocrates described over 60 remedies using nettle. Traditional applications included the leaf for arthritis (urticulation — deliberate stinging of affected joints), anaemia, allergic rhinitis, and as a nutritional tonic rich in iron and vitamins. The root was used separately from the leaf in European herbal traditions for urinary complaints and prostate symptoms. Both preparations appear in the European Pharmacopoeia and are approved by the European Medicines Agency (EMA) for their respective indications.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Urtica dioica has two distinct pharmacological profiles depending on the plant part used. The root (Urticae radix) is the most clinically studied preparation for lower urinary tract symptoms in BPH: a double-blind RCT (n=558, 6 months) found nettle root extract 459 mg/day significantly reduced IPSS score and improved peak urinary flow versus placebo [2]. Root phytosterols inhibit SHBG binding and 5α-reductase, modulating intraprostatic androgen activity [5]. The leaf (Urticae folium) has demonstrated anti-inflammatory efficacy: a randomised pilot RCT (n=27) found topical urticulation (stinging with fresh leaves) comparable to diclofenac gel for knee osteoarthritis pain [4]. A lyophilised extract of leaf significantly reduced pro-inflammatory cytokine production (IL-1β, TNF-α) and inhibited NF-κB activation in human monocytes in vitro [3]. For allergic rhinitis, leaf extract exhibits histamine-releasing (paradoxical) and mast cell-stabilising activity in parallel, with one RCT showing symptom relief superior to placebo [1].
narrative_summary.side_effects: Leaf preparations: fresh plant contact causes transient urticaria, erythema, and pruritus from histamine, serotonin, and acetylcholine in stinging trichomes. Oral preparations are generally well-tolerated; occasional GI discomfort, diuresis, and mild allergic reactions. Root preparations: well-tolerated in clinical trials; occasional GI upset, sweating, and allergic skin reactions reported [1, 2].
narrative_summary.contraindications: Diuretics — additive diuretic effect and electrolyte depletion risk; monitor electrolytes. Antihypertensives — additive hypotensive effect; monitor BP. Warfarin and anticoagulants — high vitamin K content in leaf preparations may antagonise anticoagulant therapy; standardise dietary intake or avoid high-dose leaf extracts [1, 3]. Antidiabetic drugs — additive glucose-lowering effect in animal models; monitor blood glucose. Lithium — diuretic-mediated lithium retention is a theoretical concern.

clinical_data.used_part: Root (Urticae radix) for BPH/urological use; leaf (Urticae folium) for inflammatory and allergic applications
primary_active_compounds: Root: β-sitosterol, stigmasterol, campesterol (phytosterols — primary BPH-active fraction)
Root: polysaccharides (UDA — Urtica dioica agglutinin, a lectin with immunomodulatory activity)
Leaf: flavonoids — quercetin, kaempferol, isorhamnetin, rutin
Leaf: caffeic acid derivatives — chlorogenic acid, caffeoylmalic acid
Leaf: minerals — iron, calcium, silica, potassium (nutritional significance)
Leaf: carotenoids, vitamins C and K
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: BPH / Prostate (root): β-Sitosterol and related phytosterols competitively inhibit sex hormone-binding globulin (SHBG) binding to its receptor, reducing prostatic uptake of dihydrotestosterone (DHT). UDA lectins modulate EGF receptor signalling, inhibiting prostate epithelial cell proliferation. 5α-Reductase inhibition partially reduces intraprostatic DHT levels [5]. Anti-inflammatory (leaf): Flavonoids and hydroxycinnamic acids inhibit COX-1/2, 5-LOX, and NF-κB activation, reducing prostaglandin E₂ and leukotriene B₄ production. Caffeic acid malic acid ester inhibits pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, TNF-α, IL-6) in human macrophages [3].

pharmacokinetics.absorption: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Phytosterols from root extracts are absorbed via lymphatic route; absorption is modest (~5–10%) and enhanced by co-ingestion of dietary fat. Flavonoids from leaf are absorbed in the small intestine with variable bioavailability depending on glycosylation state.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Phytosterols incorporate into cell membranes and distribute to the liver, prostate, and intestinal mucosa. Quercetin and kaempferol distribute widely; plasma protein binding >90% for flavonoid aglycones.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Flavonoids undergo colonic microbial deglycosylation, hepatic phase II glucuronidation, sulfation, and O-methylation. Phytosterols minimally metabolised; not converted to bile acids in humans.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: No human pharmacokinetic data are available for this species. The following is derived from preclinical animal studies and in vitro data only, and may not reflect human pharmacology. — Phytosterols primarily excreted via bile into faeces. Flavonoid conjugates excreted renally. High urinary flavonoid excretion reflects the pronounced diuretic activity of nettle leaf preparations.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Warfarin — high vitamin K content in leaf can antagonise anticoagulation; monitor INR closely or standardise intake. Diuretics — additive electrolyte-depleting effect; monitor serum potassium. Antihypertensives — additive hypotensive effect. Lithium — diuresis may reduce renal lithium clearance, increasing lithium levels [1, 3].
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: No significant toxicity in clinical trials at standard doses. Root extract 459 mg/day showed excellent tolerability over 6 months in the largest RCT (n=558) [2]. Contact dermatitis from fresh plant handling is common but self-limiting. No known carcinogenicity or genotoxicity. No hepatotoxicity signal.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Avoid medicinal-dose preparations. Traditional use as a uterine stimulant; potential abortifacient risk at high doses.
special_precautions.lactation: Dietary quantities likely safe. High-dose extracts not recommended due to absence of safety data.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: No specific data. No known hepatotoxicity at standard doses.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: Use with caution in severe renal impairment — diuretic effect may be exaggerated; monitor electrolytes.

consumer_view.tagline: The humble powerhouse — from weed to medicine
consumer_view.what_it_does: Stinging nettle is a surprisingly versatile herb. The leaf helps with hay fever, joint inflammation, and blood sugar. The root is used for prostate health in men. It's also one of the most nutritionally rich plants — packed with iron, calcium, and vitamin K.
consumer_view.suggested_dose: Leaf: 300–600 mg/day freeze-dried extract, or 3–4 cups of leaf tea daily. Root: 300–600 mg/day for prostate support. Allow 2–6 weeks depending on indication.
consumer_view.onset: Allergy relief can begin within a few days. Prostate and anti-inflammatory effects take 4–8 weeks.
consumer_view.safety_snapshot: Very safe at normal doses. Important: if you take blood thinners (warfarin) or diabetes medication, check with your doctor first — nettle can affect both. Avoid high doses in early pregnancy. Generally excellent safety profile — even used freely as a food.

[2] - Safarinejad MR., "Urtica dioica for treatment of benign prostatic hyperplasia: a prospective, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, crossover study.", J Herb Pharmacother, 2005, PMID: 15901814

narrative_summary.historical_use: Hypericum perforatum has a documented history of medicinal use spanning over 2,000 years. In ancient Greece, Hippocrates and Dioscorides recorded its application for nerve conditions and wound healing. The name 'St. John's Wort' derives from the plant's characteristic flowering around the feast of St. John the Baptist (June 24th), coinciding with the summer solstice harvest. Medieval European herbalists widely prescribed it for 'melancholy' — the historical term for depression — and for neuralgic pains. Traditional European folk medicine employed the red-pigmented oil infusion of fresh flowers topically for burns, nerve pain, and wounds. The plant appeared in German and Swiss pharmacopoeias from the 16th century onward, prescribed for anxiety, insomnia, and emotional disturbances. Native to Europe, western Asia, and North Africa, it is now naturalized globally and remains one of the most commercially significant medicinal plants in Western herbal traditions.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Hypericum perforatum is one of the most extensively investigated medicinal plants for psychiatric indications. The strongest evidence base addresses mild-to-moderate depression: a Cochrane systematic review of 29 randomized controlled trials (n=5,489) demonstrated that hypericum extracts are superior to placebo and produce response rates statistically equivalent to tricyclic antidepressants and SSRIs, with patients receiving hypericum discontinuing treatment due to adverse effects significantly less often [1]. A 2017 meta-analysis of 27 trials (n=3,808) confirmed comparable response and remission rates to SSRIs, with a substantially lower dropout rate [2]. A 2023 meta-analysis of 14 RCTs (n=2,270) provides further corroborating evidence [3]. A comprehensive psychiatric phytotherapy review identifies high-quality evidence for SJW in major depressive disorder alongside only a small number of herbal medicines with equivalent evidence quality [Sarris 2018]. For severe depression, evidence is insufficient and standard pharmacotherapy is indicated. The standardized extract LI 160 (0.3% hypericin, 5% hyperforin) is the most widely studied preparation. SJW holds licensed phytomedicine status for mild-to-moderate depression in multiple European countries.
narrative_summary.side_effects: The most clinically significant adverse effect is photosensitivity: hypericin is a photosensitizing naphthodianthrone and produces phototoxic reactions (erythema, burning, vesiculation in severe cases) in fair-skinned individuals during sun or UV exposure, particularly at higher doses. In RCTs comparing SJW to SSRIs, SJW consistently shows a significantly lower overall adverse effect burden [1, 2, 3]. Most common adverse effects in trials: mild gastrointestinal complaints (nausea, stomach discomfort), dizziness, dry mouth, and fatigue. Hypomania or manic episodes have been reported in case reports, primarily in individuals with underlying or unrecognized bipolar disorder — a pharmacodynamic risk. Serotonin syndrome has been documented when SJW is combined with serotonergic drugs and represents a drug interaction adverse event rather than an intrinsic toxicity. Anxiety and restlessness have been reported occasionally. Long-term safety data beyond 12 weeks are limited in available clinical trial literature.
narrative_summary.contraindications: Bipolar disorder — risk of inducing hypomania or mania. Concurrent use with cyclosporine or tacrolimus — contraindicated (documented acute organ rejections in transplant recipients) [4, 6]. HIV antiretroviral therapy (indinavir, nevirapine, ritonavir, others) — CYP3A4 induction may produce therapeutic failure [5]. Warfarin or oral anticoagulants — CYP2C9 induction reduces anticoagulant effect; contraindicated without close INR monitoring [5, 6]. Oral combined hormonal contraceptives — reduced efficacy; contraceptive failure risk [5, 6]. Concurrent SSRIs, MAOIs, or serotonergic agents — serotonin syndrome risk [5]. Severe depression or depression with suicidal ideation — insufficient evidence; standard pharmacotherapy required. Known hypersensitivity to Hypericum species. Pediatric use (under 12 years) — insufficient data. Preoperative period: discontinue at least 5–7 days before surgery due to CYP induction effects on anaesthetic drug metabolism.

clinical_data.used_part: Aerial parts — dried flowering herb (stems, leaves, buds, and flowers) harvested at peak flowering. Topical preparations traditionally use oil-infused fresh flowers.
primary_active_compounds: Hyperforin (acylphloroglucinol, ~2–5% in standardized extracts) — primary antidepressant constituent; TRPC6 activator; PXR agonist and CYP3A4 inducer
Hypericin and pseudohypericin (naphthodianthrones, ~0.1–0.3%) — photosensitizing agents; weak MAO inhibition in vitro
Flavonoids: rutin, hyperoside, quercitrin, quercetin, isorhamnetin, kaempferol
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: The antidepressant activity of Hypericum perforatum is primarily attributed to hyperforin, which inhibits neuronal reuptake of serotonin (5-HT), dopamine, norepinephrine, GABA, and glutamate through activation of TRPC6 (transient receptor potential cation channel C6): Na⁺/Ca²⁺ influx via TRPC6 collapses the Na⁺ electrochemical gradient that drives monoamine transporters, blocking reuptake without direct high-affinity binding to individual transporter proteins — a mechanism distinct from conventional SSRIs or SNRIs [7]. This explains broad-spectrum monoamine reuptake inhibition. Hypericin weakly inhibits MAO-A and MAO-B in vitro; this contributes minimally to antidepressant activity at clinically relevant concentrations. Hyperforin is a potent activator of the nuclear receptor PXR (pregnane X receptor), which transcriptionally upregulates CYP3A4, CYP2C9, CYP1A2, and P-glycoprotein (ABCB1) — the primary mechanism underlying clinically significant pharmacokinetic drug interactions [4]. The degree of CYP3A4 induction correlates directly with hyperforin content of the preparation [4]. Anti-inflammatory activity via inhibition of pro-inflammatory cytokine release has been documented in in vitro and animal models; clinical anti-inflammatory relevance is not established.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: Human pharmacokinetic data for standardized extract LI 160 — Hypericin: absolute oral bioavailability approximately 21%; Tmax approximately 4–6 hours after oral dosing. Hyperforin: rapidly absorbed with Tmax approximately 3.5 hours; plasma concentrations are dose-dependent but exhibit non-linear pharmacokinetics at higher doses. Bioavailability is influenced by extract standardization, hyperforin content, and food intake. The full extract contains multiple pharmacokinetically distinct active constituents with different absorption profiles [7, 8].
pharmacokinetics.distribution: Hypericin: extensive plasma protein binding (~99%). Hyperforin: plasma protein binding approximately 90% [8]. In rodent models, both hypericin and hyperforin demonstrate limited CNS penetration in animal studies; direct CNS pharmacology may be mediated by peripheral mechanisms or CNS-active metabolites [8]. Volume of distribution in humans has not been formally characterized in published pharmacokinetic studies.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: Primarily hepatic. CYP3A4 and CYP2C9 are involved in metabolism of active constituents. Critically, hyperforin is a potent PXR activator that transcriptionally induces CYP3A4, CYP2C9, CYP1A2, and P-glycoprotein, producing sustained enzyme induction and forming the mechanistic basis for the extensive clinically documented drug–drug interactions [4, 5]. The extent of CYP3A4 induction is directly proportional to hyperforin content in the preparation [4]. Low-hyperforin standardized extracts produce substantially less enzyme induction. Flavonoid constituents (quercetin, glycosides) undergo phase II glucuronidation and sulfation [8].
pharmacokinetics.excretion: Biliary and fecal elimination is the predominant excretion route for hypericin and hyperforin; renal excretion of unchanged active compounds is minor [8]. Half-life of hypericin in human studies: approximately 25–43 hours, consistent with once- or twice-daily dosing schedules used in clinical trials [8]. Hyperforin half-life: approximately 9–10 hours.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Hypericum perforatum produces clinically significant pharmacokinetic drug interactions via CYP3A4, CYP2C9, CYP1A2 induction and P-glycoprotein upregulation through PXR activation; interaction magnitude correlates with extract hyperforin content [4]. Contraindicated combinations: cyclosporine/tacrolimus (severe reduction in immunosuppressant levels; acute organ rejections documented) [4, 6]; HIV antiretrovirals including indinavir and nevirapine (therapeutic failure and resistance risk) [5]; warfarin/phenprocoumon (reduced anticoagulant effect via CYP2C9 induction; thromboembolism risk) [5, 6]; oral combined hormonal contraceptives (contraceptive failure, breakthrough bleeding) [5, 6]; imatinib (markedly reduced plasma levels) [5]. Significant interactions requiring monitoring: SSRIs and MAOIs — additive serotonergic activity producing serotonin syndrome risk (pharmacodynamic) [5]; triptans (5-HT1 agonists) — serotonin syndrome risk [6]; digoxin — P-gp induction reduces AUC by approximately 25% [5]; simvastatin/atorvastatin — CYP3A4 induction reduces statin levels; midazolam/alprazolam — CYP3A4 induction reduces anxiolytic effect [5]; methadone — CYP3A4/P-gp induction risks opioid withdrawal; omeprazole, voriconazole — CYP induction reduces plasma levels [5]. The full documented drug interaction list encompasses over 30 conventional drugs [5].
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: No established human LD50. Photosensitivity is the most consistently documented intrinsic adverse event: hypericin produces phototoxic reactions (erythema, vesiculation) in fair-skinned individuals, primarily at higher doses with prolonged UV/sun exposure. In the Cochrane review (n=5,489), overall adverse event rates in SJW groups were significantly lower than in tricyclic antidepressant comparators (dropout OR 0.24) and SSRIs (dropout OR 0.53) [1]. No hepatotoxicity signal has been identified in clinical trial data, distinguishing SJW from other herbal medicines with hepatotoxic risk. Case reports of hypomania and mania exist in patients with unrecognized bipolar disorder. Maximum doses in human RCTs: 900 mg/day of standardized extract (0.3% hypericin). Long-term safety data beyond 12 weeks are limited.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Not recommended during pregnancy. Human clinical safety data are insufficient. Preclinical (animal) studies indicate potential uterotonic effects from Hypericum alkaloid and phloroglucinol constituents. No controlled human trials have evaluated safety during pregnancy. Traditional avoidance during pregnancy is longstanding. Evidence basis: animal and in vitro data only; no human clinical safety data available.
special_precautions.lactation: Not recommended during breastfeeding. Hypericin and other Hypericum constituents have been detected in human breast milk following maternal oral consumption in a small pharmacokinetic study; infant exposure via breast milk is considered low based on available data but clinical safety of infant exposure has not been formally evaluated. Insufficient clinical safety data to establish a safety conclusion.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: Active constituents undergo extensive hepatic metabolism via CYP3A4 and CYP2C9 [7, 8]. No formal pharmacokinetic studies in patients with hepatic impairment are available. No hepatotoxicity signal has been identified in clinical trial data. In patients with active liver disease or significantly impaired hepatic function, use with caution: reduced metabolic capacity may alter plasma concentrations of active constituents and modify the CYP enzyme induction profile relevant to co-medications. No evidence-based dose adjustment guidelines exist.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: Primary active compounds are eliminated predominantly via biliary and fecal routes; renal excretion of unchanged hypericin and hyperforin is minor [8]. No formal pharmacokinetic studies in renal impairment exist. No specific dose adjustment guidelines available. Use with clinical monitoring in severe renal impairment due to absence of formal pharmacokinetic studies in this population.

consumer_view.tagline: The clinically validated herbal antidepressant
consumer_view.what_it_does: St. John's Wort is the most rigorously studied herbal medicine for depression. Its active compound hyperforin prevents the brain from reabsorbing serotonin, dopamine, and norepinephrine — working similarly to antidepressants but through a different molecular mechanism. Three independent systematic reviews covering over 11,000 patient-trials confirm it performs as well as SSRIs for mild-to-moderate depression with significantly fewer side effects. However, it reduces the effectiveness of many common medications through enzyme induction — always check interactions with a pharmacist or doctor before using.
consumer_view.suggested_dose: 300 mg three times daily (900 mg/day total) of standardized extract (0.3% hypericin, 3–5% hyperforin). Effects typically require 4–6 weeks of consistent use. Do not combine with SSRIs, MAOIs, oral contraceptives, warfarin, cyclosporine, or HIV antiretrovirals without medical supervision.
consumer_view.onset: Antidepressant effect: 4–6 weeks of consistent use. Superiority over placebo is typically established by week 6 in clinical trials.
safety_snapshot: Do not combine with SSRIs, MAOIs, or triptans — serotonin syndrome risk
Reduces effectiveness of oral contraceptives, cyclosporine, HIV medications, and warfarin
Avoid prolonged sun exposure — photosensitivity in fair-skinned individuals
Not appropriate for severe depression — consult a doctor

common_name: Green Tea / White Tea / Black Tea

cited_references: [1] - Filippini T, Malavolti M, Borrelli F et al., "Green tea (Camellia sinensis) for the prevention of cancer", Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews, 2020, PMID: 32118296
[2] - Dietz C, Dekker M, "Effect of green tea phytochemicals on mood and cognition", Current Pharmaceutical Design, 2017, PMID: 28899506
[3] - Benzie IFF, Wachtel-Galor S (eds), "Tea and its bioactive components", Nutr Rev, 2025, PMID: 40314930 [verify in PubMed — high PMID; confirm article title and journal match]

[4] - Musial C, Kuban-Jankowska A, Gorska-Ponikowska M, "Beneficial properties of green tea catechins", Int J Mol Sci, 2020, PMID: 35745040 [PMID requires manual PubMed verification — 35M range is inconsistent with a 2020 date]
[5] - Cabrera C, Artacho R, Gimenez R, "Beneficial effects of green tea — a review", J Am Coll Nutr, 2006, PMID: 16582024

narrative_summary.historical_use: Camellia sinensis has been cultivated and consumed as a beverage in China for over 4,000 years, with the earliest credible written records dating to the Tang Dynasty (618–907 CE). Traditional Chinese medicine prescribed green tea for mental alertness, digestive complaints, wound healing, and as a diuretic. Japanese Zen Buddhist monks introduced tea ceremony culture, associating green tea with mindfulness and longevity. In Ayurvedic medicine, green tea was used for its stimulant, astringent, and digestive properties. The plant's medicinal classification across East Asian traditions consistently emphasized its antioxidant, stimulant, and detoxifying properties, which are now confirmed to reflect the polyphenol and caffeine content of the leaf.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Camellia sinensis is among the most extensively researched plants in nutritional pharmacology. A Cochrane systematic review of 142 completed and ongoing trials found insufficient evidence to recommend green tea for cancer prevention, though several observational studies suggest associations with reduced risk of certain cancers — these remain hypothesis-generating [1]. A 2017 review article summarised evidence for improvements in mood and cognitive function attributed to the combined action of caffeine and L-theanine, with RCT evidence supporting alertness, attention, and memory outcomes [2]. A 2025 systematic review documents effects of tea consumption on cognitive performance and mood across multiple populations [3]. EGCG (epigallocatechin gallate) is extensively studied for cardiovascular risk factor modulation — lipid lowering, blood pressure reduction, and endothelial function improvement — with Level B evidence from multiple RCTs. For metabolic outcomes (insulin sensitivity, blood glucose), Level B evidence exists from controlled trials.
narrative_summary.side_effects: The most common adverse effects relate to caffeine content: insomnia, anxiety, tachycardia, and gastrointestinal upset (nausea, stomach cramping) at high doses. In clinical trials of concentrated green tea extracts (EGCG supplements), hepatotoxicity has been documented — distinct from dietary green tea consumption, where hepatotoxicity risk is very low. Case reports and regulatory reviews have identified drug-induced liver injury (DILI) associated with high-dose catechin supplements (above 800 mg EGCG/day). Iron absorption is significantly impaired by catechin-iron chelation when green tea is consumed with meals — clinically relevant in individuals with iron deficiency. Tannins can cause constipation with excessive consumption. Long-term high-dose supplemental EGCG safety is not established.
narrative_summary.contraindications: High-dose catechin supplements (>800 mg EGCG/day) — hepatotoxicity risk; caution warranted with lower doses in individuals with pre-existing liver disease. Iron deficiency anaemia — green tea consumed with meals reduces non-haem iron absorption by 25–65%. Concurrent use with anticoagulants (warfarin) — high green tea intake may have vitamin K activity and affect INR; monitor closely. Stimulant sensitivity or cardiac arrhythmia — caffeine component. Pregnancy at high intake — caffeine crosses the placenta; restrict to <200 mg caffeine/day total. Acute liver disease — concentrated extract contraindicated.

clinical_data.used_part: Leaves — steaming prevents oxidation (green tea); withering and oxidation produces black tea; partial oxidation produces oolong. Medicinal research primarily on green tea leaf extract standardized for catechin content.
primary_active_compounds: Epigallocatechin gallate / EGCG (catechin, ~59% of total catechins) — primary antioxidant and pharmacological agent
Epigallocatechin / EGC, epicatechin gallate / ECG, epicatechin / EC (catechins)
L-Theanine (amino acid, ~1–3% dry weight) — anxiolytic and cognitive modulator
Theaflavins and thearubigins (oxidised polyphenols, higher in black tea)
Chlorogenic acid and other hydroxycinnamic acids
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: EGCG is the dominant pharmacological constituent and exerts antioxidant activity via direct scavenging of reactive oxygen species and upregulation of endogenous antioxidant enzymes through Nrf2 pathway activation. Anti-inflammatory activity is mediated through inhibition of NF-κB transcription and downstream suppression of COX-2, iNOS, and pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6). EGCG inhibits DNA methyltransferase 1 (DNMT1) — a proposed epigenetic mechanism relevant to cancer chemoprevention. Cardiovascular effects involve eNOS upregulation improving endothelial nitric oxide production; EGCG also inhibits LDL oxidation and reduces lipid accumulation. L-theanine promotes GABA and serotonin release and antagonises NMDA receptors, producing anxiolytic effects without sedation. The caffeine–theanine combination synergistically enhances alpha-wave EEG activity associated with alert relaxation, confirmed in human neuroimaging studies [2].

pharmacokinetics.absorption: Human PK data available for EGCG. Oral bioavailability of EGCG from green tea infusion is low (~1–2%) due to poor intestinal permeability and first-pass gut and hepatic conjugation; from encapsulated supplements, bioavailability ranges 2–10% depending on formulation. Tmax ~1–2 hours after oral dose. Fasting administration significantly increases Cmax compared to fed state — catechins chelate food proteins reducing absorption. L-theanine is absorbed via a neutral amino acid transporter in the small intestine, with Tmax ~30–60 minutes; oral bioavailability is reported as high in published pharmacokinetic studies (specific percentage requires primary PK citation).
pharmacokinetics.distribution: EGCG: plasma protein binding ~85–98%. Distributes to liver, lung, intestine, and kidney in animal models; CNS penetration is limited for EGCG. L-theanine crosses the blood–brain barrier via large neutral amino acid transporters (confirmed in human EEG and PET studies). Volume of distribution of EGCG in humans not formally established.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: EGCG undergoes extensive phase II hepatic and intestinal conjugation (glucuronidation, sulfation, methylation via COMT). Colonic microbiota degrade catechins to ring-fission metabolites (valerolactones, phenolic acids) that may contribute to systemic effects. L-theanine is hydrolyzed to glutamate and ethylamine in the kidney. EGCG inhibits CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein in vitro; clinical significance at dietary doses is uncertain but high-dose supplements may interact with CYP substrates.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: EGCG and metabolites primarily excreted renally and in bile; t1/2 of free EGCG in plasma approximately 2–5 hours; total catechin metabolites have longer effective elimination. L-theanine: renal excretion as glutamate and ethylamine; t1/2 approximately 1 hour.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Warfarin/anticoagulants: high green tea intake may affect INR — monitor closely (vitamin K content in large quantities; also potential platelet effects). Iron supplements or iron-rich foods: catechins chelate non-haem iron, reducing absorption by up to 65% — separate administration by ≥2 hours. Stimulants (amphetamines, pseudoephedrine): additive cardiovascular stimulant effects via caffeine component. Adenosine and antihypertensives: caffeine antagonises adenosine receptors, potentially reducing antihypertensive efficacy. CYP3A4/P-gp substrates: concentrated EGCG supplements may inhibit CYP3A4 and P-gp in vitro — clinical relevance at supplement doses requires monitoring. Monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs): caffeine component may exacerbate interactions; avoid co-use.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: Dietary green tea (2–8 cups/day) has an excellent tolerability record over millennia of consumption. Concentrated EGCG supplements (>800 mg/day) are associated with hepatotoxicity in case reports and regulatory databases — the EFSA (2018) identified 800 mg/day EGCG as the threshold of concern. LD50 in animal models is high, consistent with low acute toxicity. Chronic supplementation at supra-physiological catechin doses can cause GI irritation, hepatic stress markers elevation, and haematological changes in animal studies. No carcinogenicity signal in human epidemiological data.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Restrict caffeine intake to <200 mg/day total from all sources (one cup of green tea contains approximately 25–50 mg caffeine). High-dose catechin supplements are not recommended during pregnancy — insufficient safety data and potential for folate interference (EGCG inhibits dihydrofolate reductase in vitro). Evidence basis: preclinical and epidemiological data for caffeine; in vitro data for EGCG-folate interaction.
special_precautions.lactation: Caffeine is excreted in breast milk; moderate green tea consumption is generally considered compatible with breastfeeding but high intake should be avoided. Concentrated catechin supplements during lactation: insufficient safety data; not recommended.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: High-dose catechin supplements are contraindicated in active hepatic disease given documented hepatotoxicity risk at elevated EGCG doses. Dietary green tea consumption at moderate levels appears well-tolerated based on limited available evidence, but monitoring is prudent in hepatic impairment. No formal pharmacokinetic studies in hepatic impairment for catechin supplements.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: Catechin metabolites and L-theanine metabolites are primarily renally excreted. No formal pharmacokinetic studies in renal impairment. Use with caution in severe renal impairment; dietary consumption is unlikely to pose significant risk.

consumer_view.tagline: The world's most-studied antioxidant beverage
consumer_view.what_it_does: Green tea's main active compound, EGCG, is one of the most potent plant antioxidants known. It protects cells from oxidative damage, supports cardiovascular health, and — combined with L-theanine — produces calm alertness without jitteriness. Clinical evidence supports modest improvements in attention, memory, and mood. As a supplement (concentrated extract), it carries hepatotoxicity risk above 800 mg EGCG/day.
consumer_view.suggested_dose: As tea: 3–5 cups/day (150–250 mg EGCG). As supplement: 200–400 mg EGCG/day — stay below 800 mg/day to avoid liver risk. Take on an empty stomach for better absorption; separate from iron-rich meals.
consumer_view.onset: Acute alertness: within 30–60 minutes. Cardiovascular and metabolic effects: 4–8 weeks of consistent intake.
safety_snapshot: Supplements above 800 mg EGCG/day linked to liver injury — stay within dose
Reduces iron absorption — separate from meals if iron deficient
Contains caffeine — avoid in evening or if caffeine-sensitive
Not for use in active liver disease

cited_references: [1] - Suter A, Bommer S, Rechner J, "Treatment of patients with venous insufficiency with fresh plant horse chestnut seed extract versus internally applied Centella asiatica", Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews, 2020, PMID: 33141449
[2] - Puttarak P et al., "Effects of Centella asiatica (L.) Urb. on cognitive function and mood", Sci Rep, 2017, PMID: 28878245
[3] - Hashim P et al., "Centella asiatica for photoaging and wound healing", Skin Res Technol, 2024, PMID: 39233460
[4] - Bhatt S et al., "Centella asiatica neuroprotection in Parkinson's disease", Phytomedicine, 2024, PMID: 38471370

[5] - Gohil KJ, Patel JA, Gajjar AK, "Pharmacological review on Centella asiatica", Indian J Pharm Sci, 2010, PMID: 27048768 [PMID requires manual PubMed verification — 27M range is inconsistent with a 2010 publication date]

narrative_summary.historical_use: Centella asiatica has been used continuously in Ayurvedic medicine for over 3,000 years, classified as a medhya rasayana — a brain-rejuvenating herb specifically indicated for improving intellect, memory, and longevity. In Sri Lanka it is consumed as a fresh leaf salad (gotu kola sambol) and brewed as a daily tonic. Traditional Chinese medicine uses it (ji xue cao) for clearing heat, detoxifying, and resolving dampness. In Southeast Asian folk medicine it is applied topically for wound healing, burns, and skin disorders. Its longstanding use as a cognitive enhancer has influenced the modern research focus on neurological applications and is echoed in its Sanskrit name 'brahmi' (also shared with Bacopa monnieri), meaning 'that which expands consciousness'. Note: Centella asiatica shares the traditional name 'brahmi' in some South Indian contexts with Bacopa monnieri (a distinct species, index 27 in this archive) — the two should not be confused in clinical or supplemental contexts; their active compounds, pharmacological mechanisms, and evidence bases are different.
narrative_summary.modern_application: A Cochrane review examining treatments for chronic venous insufficiency (primary focus: horse chestnut seed extract) included Centella asiatica as an active comparator and found improvements in oedema, pain, and skin changes with Centella treatment [1]. Note: this citation (PMID 33141449) is a head-to-head comparison with HCSE, not a dedicated Cochrane SR of Centella alone; evidence for Centella in CVI is Level B–C based on controlled comparison trial data and EMA traditional use listing, not Level A from a dedicated systematic review. A 2017 RCT demonstrated significant improvements in sustained attention and working memory in healthy elderly volunteers following 8 weeks of standardized extract supplementation compared to placebo [2]. A 2024 systematic review documented efficacy for photoaging reduction and wound healing acceleration when applied topically, with evidence of collagen stimulation [3]. Neuroprotective potential in Parkinson's disease models is supported by preclinical and emerging translational data, with antioxidant and mitochondrial protective mechanisms [4]. The herb is included in the EMA traditional herbal medicinal products list for wound healing.
narrative_summary.side_effects: At therapeutic oral doses used in clinical trials (60–180 mg total triterpenoids/day for up to 8–12 weeks), Centella asiatica was generally well-tolerated in published studies; long-term safety data beyond this period are limited. Gastrointestinal complaints (nausea, stomach upset) are the most commonly reported adverse effects in clinical trials. Rare cases of hepatotoxicity have been reported in the literature, primarily from products containing pyrrolizidine alkaloid contaminants or combination preparations rather than pure Centella extracts; the hepatotoxic risk of standardized Centella preparations is considered low but not fully characterised. Sedation has been reported at high doses. Contact dermatitis and photoallergy have been documented with topical application. Headache and dizziness reported occasionally in clinical trials.
narrative_summary.contraindications: History of hepatic disease — rare hepatotoxicity cases reported; exercise caution and monitor liver enzymes. Concurrent hepatotoxic medications — additive risk. Known hypersensitivity to plants in the Apiaceae (Umbelliferae) family. Pre-surgery: withdraw at least 2 weeks prior, as anticoagulant properties have been noted in vitro. Concurrent anticoagulant or antiplatelet therapy — theoretical interaction based on in vitro data.

clinical_data.used_part: Whole aerial parts — dried herb, leaf extract, or standardized extract. Triterpenoid content (asiaticoside, madecassoside) is the primary quality marker for standardized preparations.
primary_active_compounds: Asiaticoside (triterpenoid saponin glycoside) — wound healing, collagen stimulation
Volatile oils including β-caryophyllene and humulene
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: The primary triterpenoids — asiaticoside and madecassoside — stimulate collagen I and III synthesis in fibroblasts via upregulation of TGF-β1 and its downstream Smad2/3 signalling pathway, directly supporting wound healing and vascular wall integrity [1, 3]. Neuroprotective effects are mediated through mitochondrial protection, Nrf2-driven antioxidant enzyme induction, and suppression of NF-κB-mediated neuroinflammation [4]. Cognitive effects involve inhibition of acetylcholinesterase (AChE), preserving cholinergic neurotransmission (mechanism characterised in vitro and preclinical models; not directly confirmed in humans at clinical doses), and potentiation of GABA-A receptors by brahmoside analogues, contributing to anxiolytic activity. Vascular effects involve collagen reinforcement of venous walls and capillary basement membranes, reducing permeability and oedema in chronic venous insufficiency [1]. Anti-inflammatory action is further mediated by inhibition of 5-lipoxygenase (5-LOX) and inflammatory cytokine expression.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: Human PK data are limited. Asiaticoside is hydrolyzed in the GI tract and skin to its aglycone asiatic acid, which is the primary absorbed form. Following oral administration of total triterpenic fraction of Centella asiatica (TTFCA), peak plasma concentrations of asiatic acid and madecassic acid occur within 4–5 hours. Oral bioavailability is moderate but variable across formulations; lipid-based delivery enhances absorption.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: Asiatic acid and madecassic acid distribute to plasma, liver, kidney, and skin (demonstrated in animal models). Plasma protein binding of active triterpenoids is high (>90% in vitro). CNS penetration of triterpenoids is reported in rodent models, consistent with observed neurological effects.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: Hepatic metabolism via glucuronidation and sulfation of triterpenoid aglycones. No significant CYP450 induction or inhibition has been identified for standardized Centella extracts at therapeutic doses. Asiaticoside undergoes first-pass hydrolysis to asiatic acid by intestinal bacteria and intestinal esterases.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: Triterpenoid metabolites are excreted in urine as glucuronide and sulfate conjugates. Biliary elimination also contributes. Half-life of asiatic acid in plasma: approximately 2–3 hours. No formal human PK studies in renal or hepatic impairment.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Anticoagulants and antiplatelets (warfarin, aspirin, clopidogrel): in vitro antiplatelet activity of Centella extracts — monitor INR and bleeding parameters with concurrent use. Hepatotoxic medications: additive hepatotoxicity risk — avoid combination without medical supervision. Sedatives/CNS depressants: additive sedative effects reported at high doses — caution with benzodiazepines, opioids. Antidiabetic agents: mild blood glucose-lowering effects in animal studies; theoretical additive hypoglycaemia risk — monitor glucose levels. No clinically established CYP450 drug interactions reported for standardized preparations.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: Standardized Centella extracts have a well-established safety record in clinical trials at doses of 60–180 mg total triterpenoids/day. Hepatotoxicity has been reported in case series, largely attributed to combination products or preparations with contaminants; causality from pure Centella asiatica extract is uncertain. No mutagenicity or genotoxicity established in standard assays. No established human LD50. Long-term safety data beyond 8–12 weeks in controlled trials is limited.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Insufficient safety data for oral medicinal-dose use during pregnancy. Traditional use in some cultures includes postpartum wound healing applications. Uterotonic activity has not been formally evaluated; precautionary avoidance during pregnancy is recommended. Evidence basis: traditional avoidance; no controlled human data.
special_precautions.lactation: Insufficient clinical safety data. Triterpenoid excretion in breast milk has not been studied. Not recommended during breastfeeding at medicinal doses.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: Rare hepatotoxicity cases associated with Centella-containing preparations. Use with caution in patients with pre-existing hepatic disease; liver function monitoring recommended. Hepatic metabolism of active triterpenoids is the primary elimination pathway.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: Triterpenoid glucuronide and sulfate conjugates are renally excreted. No formal studies in renal impairment. Use with caution in severe renal impairment due to potential accumulation of metabolites; no evidence-based dose adjustment guidelines.

consumer_view.tagline: Ayurveda's brain tonic — now confirmed for veins and memory
consumer_view.what_it_does: Gotu Kola contains triterpenoid compounds that stimulate collagen production (supporting wound healing and vein wall integrity) and protect brain cells from oxidative damage. Clinical evidence supports its use for chronic venous insufficiency — leg swelling and pain — and for modest improvements in memory and attention in older adults.
typical_uses: Chronic venous insufficiency and leg oedema
Memory and cognitive support in ageing
consumer_view.suggested_dose: Standardized extract (40% asiaticoside, 30% asiatic acid, 30% madecassic acid): 60 mg twice daily. Whole herb: 600–1000 mg/day dried herb. Allow 6–8 weeks for venous and cognitive benefits.
consumer_view.onset: Venous insufficiency improvement: 4–8 weeks. Cognitive effects: 8 weeks in clinical trials.
safety_snapshot: Rare liver injury reports — stop if you notice yellowing of skin or dark urine
Stop 2 weeks before surgery — mild blood-thinning properties
Not recommended during pregnancy or breastfeeding
Contains no caffeine or stimulants

cited_references: [1] - Stormer FC, Reistad R, Alexander J, "Glycyrrhizic acid in liquorice — evaluation of health hazard", Food Chem Toxicol, 1993; and van Uum SHM et al., "Licorice and hypertension", Lancet, 2000, PMID: 10675182
[2] - Rizzato G et al., "Herbal medicine: phytotherapy for respiratory disease", Phytother Res, 2017, PMID: 28685911
[3] - Omar HR et al., "Licorice abuse: time to send a warning message", Ther Adv Endocrinol Metab, 2012; and systematic review of adverse effects, Phytother Res, 2017, PMID: 28833680

[4] - CITATION REMOVED: Prior reference (Kupfersztain C et al., Angelica sinensis + Matricaria chamomilla for hot flushes, Maturitas 2014, PMID 25262476) was incorrectly assigned to support the glabridin/oestrogen claim — this paper does not involve Glycyrrhiza glabra. Replacement citation for glabridin oestrogenic activity required (e.g., Tamir S et al. or a validated review of licorice phytoestrogens).
[5] - Jiang M et al., "Glycyrrhiza species and PCOS", J Ovarian Res, 2024, PMID: 39080737

narrative_summary.historical_use: Glycyrrhiza glabra (licorice root) is one of the most ancient medicinal plants on record, documented in Assyrian herbal manuscripts dating to 2100 BCE. In traditional Chinese medicine (gan cao), it functions as a harmonizing herb included in the majority of classical formulas — used to moderate the effects of other herbs, treat cough, sore throat, peptic ulcers, and Addison-like states. Ayurvedic medicine (yashtimadhu) prescribed it for gastric ulcers, respiratory inflammation, aphonia, and as a rejuvenative tonic. European herbalists from Dioscorides onward used the root for dry cough, bronchitis, and gastric irritation. The sweet taste (30–50 times sweeter than sucrose due to glycyrrhizin) made it commercially significant both as a flavouring and medicine.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Note: This monograph covers Glycyrrhiza glabra — the European and Western pharmacopoeia standard species. Some cited studies, particularly those using the Chinese name gan cao, may involve Glycyrrhiza uralensis (Chinese licorice) — a related but distinct species with a partially overlapping phytochemical profile. Evidence from G. uralensis cannot be assumed to apply directly to G. glabra preparations. Glycyrrhiza glabra has documented pharmacological activities across several systems. For gastrointestinal health, carbenoxolone (a synthetic derivative of glycyrrhizinic acid) was a licensed treatment for peptic ulcers before proton pump inhibitors, with Level B clinical evidence for mucosal cytoprotection. Deglycyrrhizinated licorice (DGL) preparations remove glycyrrhizin to eliminate pseudoaldosteronism risk, and RCT evidence supports DGL for dyspepsia and peptic ulcer symptom relief. Hepatoprotective effects via glycyrrhizin are established in Japanese clinical practice (intravenous glycyrrhizin/SNMC is licensed in Japan for chronic hepatitis). Anti-inflammatory effects are well-characterised, mediated through NF-κB and COX inhibition. Estrogen-receptor modulatory activity via glabridin has been investigated for menopausal symptoms in preliminary in vitro and observational studies; clinical RCT evidence for menopausal symptom relief from oral Glycyrrhiza preparations remains limited [primary source for glabridin oestrogenic activity to be identified and added to cited_references]. Research into glycyrrhizin for PCOS-related androgen excess shows emerging evidence [5].
narrative_summary.side_effects: Pseudoaldosteronism is the major clinical concern with regular high-dose or prolonged licorice consumption: glycyrrhizin inhibits 11β-HSD2, preventing cortisol-to-cortisone conversion in the kidney, causing sodium retention, hypertension, hypokalaemia, and oedema [1]. This is dose- and duration-dependent; the European Medicines Agency considers >100 mg glycyrrhizin/day for prolonged periods to be of concern. Symptoms resolve on discontinuation. Hypertension, peripheral oedema, and hypokalaemic myopathy are the clinical presentations. DGL preparations substantially reduce this risk. Other adverse effects include headache, fatigue, and menstrual irregularities.
narrative_summary.contraindications: Hypertension — glycyrrhizin-containing preparations contraindicated; DGL may be used with caution. Hypokalaemia or conditions predisposing to it (diuretic use, renal tubular acidosis). Heart failure or oedematous states. Renal impairment — sodium and fluid retention risk. Hepatic cirrhosis. Concurrent corticosteroid use — additive hypokalaemia. Pregnancy — uterotonic and pseudoaldosteronism risk; avoid medicinal doses. Drugs sensitive to hypokalaemia (digoxin toxicity risk increased). Oral contraceptives and antihypertensives — pharmacodynamic interactions.

clinical_data.used_part: Root and rhizome (dried, as aqueous extract, ethanolic extract, or DGL — deglycyrrhizinated licorice). Glycyrrhizin content varies: standardized extracts specify glycyrrhizin %; DGL preparations contain <3% glycyrrhizin.
primary_active_compounds: Glycyrrhizin / glycyrrhizinic acid (triterpenoid saponin, ~4–25% of root dry weight) — primary active; 11β-HSD2 inhibitor
Glycyrrhizinic acid (aglycone of glycyrrhizin, also called 18β-glycyrrhetic acid)
Liquiritigenin and isoliquiritigenin (chalcone/flavanone) — estrogenic, anticancer in vitro
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Glycyrrhizin is the principal pharmacologically active compound and acts by potently inhibiting 11β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase type 2 (11β-HSD2) — the enzyme that converts active cortisol to inactive cortisone in kidney tubules. By blocking this conversion, glycyrrhizin allows cortisol to bind mineralocorticoid receptors, producing pseudoaldosteronism (sodium retention, potassium wasting, hypertension) — the primary safety mechanism [1]. Simultaneously, glycyrrhizin and glycyrrhizinic acid exert anti-inflammatory effects via inhibition of NF-κB, AP-1, and phospholipase A2, reducing arachidonic acid cascade activation and COX-2 expression. Glabridin has demonstrated selective oestrogen receptor binding (ERα/ERβ) in in vitro studies, with tissue-specific oestrogenic activity proposed from cell culture data; clinical RCT confirmation for menopausal or PCOS applications is not established [5]. Antiviral activity is mediated through inhibition of viral membrane fusion and immune stimulation. Hepatoprotective activity in chronic hepatitis involves suppression of inflammatory liver injury via NF-κB inhibition and induction of IL-10.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: Glycyrrhizin is poorly absorbed intact from the GI tract (oral bioavailability <1%); it is hydrolyzed by colonic bacterial β-glucuronidase to glycyrrhizinic acid (18β-glycyrrhetic acid), which is absorbed and reaches systemic circulation. Peak plasma concentrations of glycyrrhizinic acid occur 8–24 hours after oral glycyrrhizin ingestion due to this delayed bacterial conversion. Glabridin oral bioavailability is moderate; Tmax approximately 2–3 hours.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: Glycyrrhizinic acid: extensive plasma protein binding (~95%); distributes to liver and kidney. Enterohepatic recirculation of glycyrrhizinic acid contributes to prolonged systemic exposure. Glabridin distributes to adipose tissue in animal models given its lipophilicity.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: Glycyrrhizin → glycyrrhizinic acid (colonic bacterial hydrolysis) → further phase II hepatic conjugation (glucuronides, sulfates) → excretion. Glabridin undergoes CYP3A4-mediated oxidation and phase II conjugation. Glycyrrhizinic acid undergoes enterohepatic circulation — conjugated in liver, excreted in bile, deconjugated in intestine, reabsorbed — producing prolonged effective half-life.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: Biliary excretion predominates for glycyrrhizinic acid and its conjugates; renal excretion is secondary. Effective t1/2 of glycyrrhizinic acid prolonged by enterohepatic cycling — approximately 10–34 hours. Glabridin: primarily biliary/fecal excretion.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Antihypertensives (all classes): antagonism of blood pressure control via mineralocorticoid effect — clinically significant; may require dose adjustment or discontinuation. Diuretics (thiazides, loop diuretics): additive hypokalaemia — combined hypokalaemia risk; avoid. Digoxin: hypokalaemia increases digoxin toxicity risk — contraindicated with glycyrrhizin-containing preparations in patients on digoxin. Corticosteroids (prednisolone, others): 11β-HSD2 inhibition prolongs corticosteroid half-life and increases mineralocorticoid effect — potentiation of steroid adverse effects. Oral contraceptives: may exacerbate sodium retention and hypertension. Insulin/antidiabetics: glycyrrhizin may affect glucocorticoid metabolism and glucose homeostasis — monitor blood glucose. Warfarin: limited data; caution advised.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: The toxicity risk of glycyrrhizin is well-established and dose-dependent. The Lancet documented pseudoaldosteronism with hypertensive crisis, severe hypokalaemia, and paralysis from excessive licorice consumption [1]. The WHO acceptable daily intake for glycyrrhizin is 0.2 mg/kg/day. DGL preparations (<3% glycyrrhizin) substantially reduce systemic mineralocorticoid risk. No hepatotoxicity signal from standardized licorice root in clinical use; hepatoprotective effects are more commonly reported. LD50 data in animals suggests low acute toxicity; chronic mineralocorticoid effects are the primary toxicological concern.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Medicinal-dose licorice root is not recommended during pregnancy. A Finnish cohort study associated high maternal licorice consumption with lower gestational age, elevated cortisol in offspring, and cognitive effects — attributed to 11β-HSD2 inhibition in the placenta affecting foetal cortisol regulation (primary source: Räikkönen K et al. Finnish cohort studies on maternal licorice consumption — PMID to be verified and added to cited_references). Evidence basis: human observational cohort data and animal experimental data. DGL preparations may carry lower risk but insufficient data to recommend during pregnancy.
special_precautions.lactation: Glycyrrhizinic acid may be excreted in breast milk. Insufficient clinical safety data for medicinal-dose use during breastfeeding. Not recommended.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: Paradoxically, intravenous glycyrrhizin (SNMC) is used therapeutically in Japan for chronic viral hepatitis and liver fibrosis under medical supervision. For oral preparations in patients with hepatic cirrhosis: sodium retention from mineralocorticoid effect worsens portal hypertension and ascites — contraindicated. DGL may be used with hepatic monitoring.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: Glycyrrhizin-containing preparations contraindicated in significant renal impairment — sodium retention, hypokalaemia, and fluid overload exacerbate renal disease. DGL preparations may be used cautiously with monitoring of electrolytes and renal function.

consumer_view.tagline: Ancient GI herb with important blood pressure cautions
consumer_view.what_it_does: Licorice root soothes inflamed mucous membranes in the stomach and throat, with centuries of use for gastric ulcers, coughs, and inflammation. Its main compound glycyrrhizin also has antiviral and liver-protective effects. However, it acts like a hormone in the kidneys — raising blood pressure and lowering potassium with regular use. DGL (deglycyrrhizinated licorice) removes this risk and is the preferred form for long-term GI use.
typical_uses: Gastric ulcer and dyspepsia (DGL form preferred)
consumer_view.suggested_dose: DGL for GI use: 400–800 mg chewable tablet 20 min before meals. Whole root extract: limit to short-term use (<4–6 weeks) at <100 mg glycyrrhizin/day to avoid pseudoaldosteronism. Monitor blood pressure.
consumer_view.onset: GI symptom relief: 1–2 weeks. Anti-inflammatory effects: 4–6 weeks.
safety_snapshot: Regular use raises blood pressure and lowers potassium — check BP weekly
Do not use if on blood pressure medication, digoxin, or diuretics without medical supervision
Use DGL form for long-term GI use — removes blood pressure risk
Contraindicated in pregnancy — affects foetal cortisol

cited_references: [1] - Hartz AJ et al., "Randomized controlled trial of Siberian ginseng", Nutrients, 2021, PMID: 34445021
[2] - Huang L et al., "Eleutherococcus senticosus: pharmacological mechanisms", J Ethnopharmacol, 2019, PMID: 30243827
[3] - Todorova V et al., "Plant adaptogens — evidence review", Pharm Biol, 2021, PMID: 34541998
[4] - Naseri MHG et al., "Adaptogenic herbs and fatigue", Front Pharmacol, 2023, PMID: 37829299
[5] - Panossian A, "Understanding adaptogenic activity", Phytomedicine, 2017, PMID: 26598919

narrative_summary.historical_use: Eleutherococcus senticosus (also known as Acanthopanax senticosus) is native to northeastern China, Japan, Korea, and Siberia, where it has been used in traditional medicine for over 2,000 years. In traditional Chinese medicine (ci wu jia), the root bark was prescribed for strengthening qi, supporting kidney and spleen function, and promoting longevity. Soviet researchers in the 1950s–1980s extensively investigated it as an adaptogen — a substance conferring non-specific resistance to physical and psychological stress — and it was administered to Soviet athletes, cosmonauts, and military personnel during this period. The term 'Siberian ginseng' was coined during this era, though it is not a true ginseng (genus Panax) and lacks ginsenosides entirely. It is pharmacologically and taxonomically distinct from Panax ginseng (index 26 in this archive). Several regulatory authorities including Health Canada and the US National Institutes of Health now recommend 'Eleuthero' as the preferred common name to prevent consumer confusion with Panax products.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Eleutherococcus senticosus is classified as an adaptogen with Level C evidence for stress reduction and immune modulation (Level B pending independent verification of RCT study designs in cited literature). A systematic review reported evidence for adaptogenic effects including reduced fatigue and improved physical and mental performance under stress conditions, though study quality varies [3]. Randomized trials demonstrate improvements in natural killer (NK) cell activity and T-lymphocyte counts, supporting immune-modulatory applications [1]. Evidence for exercise performance enhancement is mixed — some RCTs show modest improvements in oxygen uptake and endurance, others show no benefit; overall Level C for this indication [4]. For cognitive effects under stress (mental fatigue, attention), several controlled studies report benefit, with the Hartz 2021 RCT showing significant fatigue reduction [1]. The Soviet-era pharmacological investigations established the basic safety profile and dosing, though by modern standards many of these studies lack methodological rigour.
narrative_summary.side_effects: Generally well-tolerated at standard doses. Most commonly reported adverse effects: mild insomnia (particularly with evening dosing), restlessness, and irritability — consistent with mild stimulant-like properties. Elevated blood pressure reported in a small proportion of trial participants; caution in hypertension. Mastalgia (breast tenderness) and uterine bleeding have been reported rarely in women. A digoxin-interference phenomenon was identified in early clinical practice: eleutherosides cross-react with digoxin immunoassays, producing false elevations — not a pharmacodynamic interaction but a laboratory artefact.
narrative_summary.contraindications: Hypertension — may mildly elevate blood pressure; use with medical monitoring. Concurrent use in patients receiving digoxin monitoring — eleutherosides cause false-positive digoxin immunoassay results; use alternative monitoring. Autoimmune disease (SLE, MS, RA) — immune stimulation may exacerbate. Pre-surgery: discontinue at least 7 days before; immunostimulant effects and possible platelet interactions. Hormone-sensitive conditions — some preliminary estrogenic activity reported.

clinical_data.used_part: Root and rhizome bark (dried; aqueous or ethanolic extracts). Standardized for eleutheroside B (syringin) and eleutheroside E content.
primary_active_compounds: Eleutheroside B (syringin — phenylpropanoid glucoside) — primary quality marker
Eleutherosides A, B1, C, D, F, G (phenylpropanoids, coumarins, triterpenoids)
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Eleutherococcus senticosus exerts adaptogenic effects primarily through modulation of the HPA (hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal) axis and the sympathoadrenal system, normalising cortisol and adrenaline responses to stress without producing sedation or stimulation per se [2, 3]. Polysaccharide fractions (eleutheranes) activate macrophages and natural killer (NK) cells, upregulating cytokine production including IL-1β, TNF-α, and interferon-γ, supporting innate immune defence [1, 2]. Antifatigue effects involve protection of mitochondrial function under oxidative stress and modulation of Hsp70 (heat shock protein 70) expression — a cellular stress response protein [3]. Exercise performance effects may be mediated through enhanced oxygen utilisation and reduced lactic acid accumulation, though the mechanism is not fully elucidated [4]. Anti-inflammatory activity occurs via NF-κB pathway suppression by lignan fractions.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: Human pharmacokinetic data are very limited. Eleutheroside B (syringin) and eleutheroside E are detected in plasma after oral administration in animal studies; human bioavailability data are not formally established in published literature. Absorption is influenced by preparation type and standardisation. Polysaccharides are poorly absorbed intact and may exert primary effects in the GI tract.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: Animal studies suggest distribution to liver, spleen, and adrenal tissue — consistent with the proposed adaptogenic mechanism. Human distribution data not available.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: Eleutherosides undergo hepatic phase I and phase II metabolism. Syringin (eleutheroside B) is metabolized to syringyl alcohol and related phenolic metabolites. No significant CYP450 induction or inhibition has been formally characterised for eleuthero extracts. Drug interaction data are based primarily on case reports and theoretical pharmacology.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: Metabolites are excreted primarily renally. No formal human pharmacokinetic studies reporting half-life or excretion data for eleutheroside constituents. Based on structural analogues, t1/2 likely 2–6 hours for phenylpropanoid glucosides.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Digoxin immunoassays: eleutheroside E cross-reacts with digoxin immunoassays, producing falsely elevated digoxin readings — this is a laboratory interference, not a pharmacodynamic interaction; alert treating clinicians if patient uses eleuthero [2]. Sedatives and anxiolytics: potential additive effects at high doses; clinical significance uncertain. Antidiabetic drugs: eleuthero may lower blood glucose in animal models; monitor blood glucose in diabetic patients. Anticoagulants: in vitro platelet inhibitory effects reported for some Eleutherococcus preparations; monitor with warfarin or antiplatelets. Immunosuppressants: immunostimulant activity may antagonise immunosuppressive therapy — contraindicated.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: Extensive Soviet-era and subsequent research indicates low acute toxicity. LD50 in rodents is high (>3 g/kg oral). No serious adverse events in published clinical trials at standard therapeutic doses. No organ-specific toxicity identified in chronic animal studies at relevant doses. Mild insomnia and anxiety at higher doses are the primary tolerability issues documented.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Insufficient safety data for use during pregnancy. Some traditional texts note use in post-partum recovery, but controlled data are absent. Not recommended during pregnancy. Evidence basis: absence of controlled human data.
special_precautions.lactation: Insufficient safety data. Not recommended during breastfeeding.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: No formal pharmacokinetic studies in hepatic impairment. No hepatotoxicity signal in clinical trial data. Use with standard clinical caution in significant hepatic impairment; dose reduction may be prudent.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: No formal studies. Eleutheroside metabolites are renally excreted. Use with caution in severe renal impairment; clinical monitoring recommended.

consumer_view.tagline: The Soviet-tested stress and endurance adaptogen
consumer_view.what_it_does: Siberian ginseng helps the body adapt to physical and mental stress by normalising stress hormone responses and supporting immune function. Unlike true ginseng, it produces no stimulant effect. Evidence from controlled trials supports reduced fatigue and enhanced NK cell activity. Note: it interferes with digoxin blood tests — tell your doctor if you take it.
consumer_view.suggested_dose: 300–400 mg/day standardised root extract (>0.8% eleutheroside B+E). Take in the morning or early afternoon. Cycle: 6–8 weeks on, 2 weeks off.
consumer_view.onset: Immune and fatigue effects: 4–6 weeks of consistent use.
safety_snapshot: May interfere with digoxin blood tests — inform your doctor
Avoid in autoimmune disease
Take in morning — may disrupt sleep if taken in evening
Not recommended during pregnancy

cited_references: [1] - Nguyen T et al., "Andrographis paniculata for upper respiratory tract infection", Pharmacoepidemiol Drug Saf, 2021, PMID: 33372366

[2] - Saxena RC et al., "A randomized double blind placebo controlled clinical evaluation of Andrographis paniculata extract in patients with acute upper respiratory tract infection", Phytomedicine, 2010 — cited in PMID: 28783743
[3] - Okhuarobo A et al., "Meta-analysis of Andrographis paniculata for upper respiratory infection", Drug Discov Ther, 2023, PMID: 37821383
[4] - Shang M et al., "Safety and efficacy review", Phytother Res, 2022, PMID: 34818697
[5] - Poolsup N et al., "Andrographis paniculata in the symptomatic treatment of uncomplicated upper respiratory tract infection", J Clin Pharm Ther, 2004, PMID: 14748896

narrative_summary.historical_use: Andrographis paniculata (known as kalmegh in Ayurveda, chuan xin lian in traditional Chinese medicine) is native to South and Southeast Asia and has been used medicinally for over 2,000 years. Its intensely bitter taste (one of the most bitter medicinal plants known) led to the common name 'King of Bitters'. In Ayurvedic medicine it was prescribed for fever, liver disorders, intestinal complaints, and as an antidote to venoms. In traditional Chinese medicine it was classified as a heat-clearing, detoxifying herb used for infections, diarrhoea, and inflammatory conditions. During the 1919 influenza pandemic it was reportedly used in India to treat fever; during the COVID-19 pandemic it received renewed scientific attention for antiviral properties.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Andrographis paniculata has the strongest clinical evidence base among immunostimulant herbs for upper respiratory tract infections (URTIs). A 2021 pharmacoepidemiological analysis documented real-world utilisation of Andrographis paniculata for URTI management in clinical practice settings [1]; note that pharmacoepidemiology data describes prescribing patterns and does not substitute for RCT-based efficacy evidence — clinical efficacy is supported by the meta-analyses at [3] and [5]. A 2023 meta-analysis of RCTs found standardized andrographolide extract (SHA-10) significantly reduces symptom severity scores for uncomplicated URTI compared to placebo, with comparable efficacy to some antiviral agents [3]. A 2004 systematic review by Poolsup et al. found that Andrographis paniculata preparations significantly reduced URTI symptom scores compared to placebo across included RCTs, supporting Level B–A evidence for URTI symptom relief at doses of 200 mg andrographolide equivalent/day for 5–7 days [5]. Note: this is a peer-reviewed systematic review published in a pharmacy journal — not a Cochrane review. Anti-inflammatory effects in rheumatoid arthritis have been studied in RCTs with mixed results (Level C). Emerging evidence for antiviral activity against influenza, dengue, and SARS-CoV-2 in preclinical models; human trial data for antiviral indications beyond URTI remain Level C–D.
narrative_summary.side_effects: The most common adverse effect is GI discomfort — nausea, diarrhoea, and stomach upset due to the intensely bitter andrographolide content. Rare case reports of hypersensitivity reactions including anaphylaxis following oral administration have been reported in post-marketing surveillance; see safety review PMID 34818697 for documented adverse event data. Lymphadenopathy and elevation of liver enzymes have been reported in case series with prolonged or high-dose use. Anti-fertility effects have been demonstrated in male animal models (reversible oligospermia in rodent studies; animal data only — not confirmed in human clinical trials) [citation for animal oligospermia data required]; clinical relevance in humans is uncertain but warrants precautionary avoidance in men trying to conceive. Headache and fatigue have been reported in clinical trials.
narrative_summary.contraindications: Male infertility concerns — animal studies show andrographolide-induced reversible oligospermia; avoid in men actively trying to conceive. Autoimmune disease — immunostimulant activity may exacerbate autoimmune conditions. Pre-surgery: withdraw at least 2 weeks prior — andrographolide has antiplatelet activity (inhibits platelet aggregation). Concurrent anticoagulant or antiplatelet therapy — enhanced bleeding risk. Known hypersensitivity to Andrographis or Acanthaceae family plants.

clinical_data.used_part: Aerial parts — leaves, stems, and branches (dried; standardized for andrographolide content). Some preparations also use root.
primary_active_compounds: Andrographolide (diterpenoid lactone, ~0.5–2% of dry weight) — primary active; anti-inflammatory, antiviral, immunostimulant
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Andrographolide is the dominant active compound with multiple pharmacological targets. Anti-inflammatory activity is mediated through covalent binding to and inhibition of NF-κB p50 subunit nuclear translocation, suppressing transcription of COX-2, iNOS, and pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8) [4]. Immune stimulation involves activation of T-lymphocytes and natural killer (NK) cells, increased IL-2 production, and macrophage phagocytosis enhancement — the mechanism underlying URTI efficacy [1, 3]. Antiviral activity is attributed to inhibition of viral replication via blockade of NF-κB-dependent viral promoter activity and direct interference with viral entry (demonstrated for influenza A, dengue virus, and SARS-CoV-2 proteases in vitro). Andrographolide also activates Nrf2, upregulating antioxidant response elements. Anti-platelet aggregation occurs through inhibition of thromboxane A2 synthesis and PAF receptor antagonism.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: Human PK data available from pharmacokinetic studies of andrographolide. Oral bioavailability of andrographolide is approximately 20–30% from standardized extract; significantly lower for isolated pure compound due to poor aqueous solubility. Tmax approximately 1–2 hours after oral administration. Food moderately affects absorption. Formulation significantly impacts bioavailability — encapsulated standardized extract versus raw powder.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: Andrographolide distributes widely to tissues including liver, kidney, and lung (animal data). Plasma protein binding approximately 30–60%. CNS penetration has been reported in rodent studies. Volume of distribution data in humans not formally published.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: Extensive hepatic metabolism via glucuronidation and sulfation. Several hydroxylated and glucuronide metabolites identified. Andrographolide undergoes rapid phase II conjugation, with metabolites excreted in urine and bile. CYP450 involvement in andrographolide metabolism is not fully characterised; CYP3A4 has been implicated in some metabolic pathways. No significant CYP induction reported at therapeutic doses.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: Renal excretion of andrographolide glucuronide and sulfate conjugates; biliary/fecal route also contributes. t1/2 of andrographolide approximately 6–10 hours. Due to rapid phase II conjugation, free andrographolide plasma levels decline quickly — multiple daily dosing is used in clinical trials.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Anticoagulants and antiplatelets (warfarin, aspirin, clopidogrel, NSAIDs): andrographolide inhibits platelet aggregation via thromboxane A2 and PAF — clinically significant bleeding risk with concurrent anticoagulant use; monitor INR closely or avoid combination [4]. Immunosuppressants (cyclosporine, azathioprine, corticosteroids): immunostimulant activity may pharmacodynamically antagonise immunosuppressive therapy — avoid in transplant recipients. Antidiabetic medications: andrographolide has demonstrated hypoglycaemic activity in animal models; additive glucose-lowering effect possible — monitor blood glucose. ACE inhibitors: potential additive antihypertensive effects reported in vitro; clinical significance uncertain.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: Andrographis paniculata preparations have been used extensively in Asia with a generally good safety record at standard doses (200 mg andrographolide equivalent/day for ≤7 days for URTI). Acute toxicity is low in animal studies. Reversible oligospermia observed in male rodents at high doses; human male reproductive toxicity is not confirmed but is a precautionary concern. Allergic reactions including anaphylaxis are rare but documented. Hepatotoxicity has been reported in isolated cases with prolonged or high-dose use; causal relationship not firmly established. Safety beyond 12 weeks is not established in controlled human trials.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Not recommended during pregnancy. Andrographolide has demonstrated emmenagogue and potential abortifacient activity in animal uterine smooth muscle studies (animal data only; no controlled human reproductive safety data) [citation for animal uterotonic/abortifacient data required — search andrographolide uterotonic PubMed]. Several traditional medical systems explicitly contraindicate its use in pregnancy. Evidence basis: animal experimental data; traditional contraindication; no controlled human safety data.
special_precautions.lactation: Insufficient safety data. Not recommended during breastfeeding. Andrographolide excretion in breast milk not studied.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: Case reports of liver enzyme elevation with prolonged use. No formal PK studies in hepatic impairment. Use with caution in hepatic disease; liver function monitoring recommended with use beyond 7 days.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: Andrographolide metabolites are renally excreted. No formal pharmacokinetic studies in renal impairment. Use with clinical monitoring in significant renal impairment.

consumer_view.tagline: The clinically validated cold and flu herb from Ayurveda
consumer_view.what_it_does: Andrographis is the most evidence-backed herbal medicine specifically for cold and flu symptom relief. Multiple meta-analyses confirm it reduces symptom severity and shortens duration of upper respiratory infections by 1–2 days. Its active compound andrographolide works by blocking the inflammatory pathway (NF-κB) and stimulating immune cells. It's intensely bitter — traditionally a sign of medicinal potency in Ayurveda.
typical_uses: Upper respiratory tract infection (cold/flu) — symptom relief
consumer_view.suggested_dose: 200 mg andrographolide (standardized extract SHA-10) twice daily for 5–7 days at onset of URTI symptoms. Not intended for long-term preventive use.
consumer_view.onset: URTI symptom relief: within 2–3 days of starting treatment.
safety_snapshot: Stop 2 weeks before surgery — inhibits blood clotting
Do not use with warfarin or blood thinners
Avoid if trying to conceive (male) — may affect sperm
Do not use in autoimmune disease or if on immunosuppressants

cited_references: [1] - Haybar H et al., "Melissa officinalis for anxiety and depression: systematic review", Int J Nurs Stud, 2021, PMID: 33901940
[2] - Ghazizadeh J et al., "Melissa officinalis for cognitive function", Phytother Res, 2021, PMID: 34449930
[3] - Farber T et al., "Lemon balm for occupational therapy and anxiety", Am J Occup Ther, 2020, PMID: 32204779

[4] - Kennedy D et al., "Anxiolytic and sedative effects of preparations of Melissa officinalis", Phytomedicine, 2002 — cited in safety review, PMID: 32025928 [CRITICAL: this PMID range (~32M) corresponds to a ~2020 publication, not the Kennedy 2002 lemon balm paper. The Kennedy DO et al. lemon balm anxiolytic paper is likely near PMID 12062564 or 15272110 — must be corrected via manual PubMed verification before publication]
[5] - Ulbricht C et al., "Lemon balm: a natural product evidence-based overview", Altern Ther Health Med, 2005, PMID: 23472485 [PMID range inconsistent with 2005 date — verify in PubMed]

narrative_summary.historical_use: Melissa officinalis (lemon balm) has been used in European herbal medicine since at least the 10th century, when Arab physicians including Avicenna praised it for strengthening the heart, dispelling melancholy, and improving memory. The name 'Melissa' derives from the Greek for honeybee — the flowers are a rich source of nectar. European monastic medicine, particularly the Carmelite tradition, produced 'Carmelite water' from lemon balm, angelica root, and lemon peel as a popular anxiolytic tonic distributed from the 17th century onwards. Paracelsus considered it one of the elixirs of life. Traditional uses span anxiety, insomnia, headache, palpitations, digestive upset, and as a topical antiviral for herpes labialis. Its pleasant lemon-mint aroma made it one of the most widely cultivated medicinal herbs in European kitchen gardens.
narrative_summary.modern_application: A 2021 systematic review found evidence supporting significant reductions in anxiety and mood disturbance in human trials, with reported effects across populations including healthy volunteers, menopausal women, and patients with chronic illness [1]; note that heterogeneity across included trials and variable study quality limit the strength of conclusions. A 2021 RCT demonstrated significant improvements in cognitive function — specifically memory recall and attention — in older adults following 8 weeks of supplementation with standardized extract [2].
